proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Whole-Food Plant-Based Dietary Pattern Screener Using Ecological Community Metrics: Application to Type 2 Diabetes Risk in the Nurses' Health Study II
proposals.motivation: Current dietary assessment often focuses on isolated nutrients or single foods, which fails to capture the synergistic complexity of whole dietary patterns. Pre-2026 evidence strongly links plant-based diets to reduced risk of chronic diseases like type 2 diabetes and certain cancers, but existing screeners lack a holistic, quantitative method to characterize the structural adequacy of a whole-food vegan pattern. A novel metric is needed to move beyond simple frequency counts to assess the diversity and balance of core plant-food groups.
proposals.hypothesis: A dietary screener based on ecological community metrics (diversity, evenness, and composition) can effectively characterize a healthful whole-food vegan pattern, and a higher score on this screener will be associated with a lower risk of incident type 2 diabetes in a prospective cohort.
proposals.method: We will adapt ecological indices (Shannon Diversity Index, Pielou's Evenness Index) to dietary data. Using pre-existing food frequency questionnaire (FFQ) data from the Nurses' Health Study II, we will define core plant-food 'guilds' (legumes, whole grains, fruits, vegetables, nuts/seeds). The screener will calculate a composite score integrating the diversity of guilds consumed, the evenness of their intake, and adherence to recommended compositional ratios (e.g., legumes-to-grains). This pattern score will be validated against nutrient adequacy and used in Cox proportional hazards models to estimate diabetes risk, adjusting for physical activity and other confounders.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, validated tool for rapidly assessing the structural quality of plant-based diets in large-scale epidemiological research. It will offer a new paradigm for dietary pattern analysis, shifting focus from isolated components to systemic properties, and generate evidence on how the ecological structure of a vegan diet relates to diabetes risk.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel by directly applying well-established ecological community analysis techniques to human nutrition, moving beyond traditional dietary indices to quantify the 'ecosystem health' of an individual's diet based on its internal structure and balance.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Machine Learning-Derived Short Screener for Adherence to a Whole-Food, Plant-Based Dietary Pattern in the Nurses' Health Study II and Health Professionals Follow-Up Study
proposals.motivation: Whole-food, plant-based (WFPB) diets are associated with reduced risk of major chronic diseases, including type 2 diabetes and certain cancers. However, assessing adherence to this holistic dietary pattern in large-scale research and clinical practice remains challenging, as existing tools are often lengthy or based on nutrient-level rather than food-pattern data. A validated, brief screener derived from real-world dietary data is urgently needed to efficiently evaluate WFPB diet quality in epidemiological and interventional studies.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning algorithms applied to detailed food frequency questionnaire (FFQ) data can identify a minimal set of food group items and consumption frequencies that accurately predict overall adherence to a WFPB dietary pattern, as defined by a validated a priori index or biomarker profile.
proposals.method: We will use dietary data from the Nurses' Health Study II and Health Professionals Follow-Up Study cohorts. A reference WFPB pattern score will be defined using established indices (e.g., plant-based diet indices) or biomarkers (e.g., plasma carotenoids). We will train and compare multiple machine learning models, including random forests, LASSO regression, and gradient boosting machines (XGBoost), on FFQ data to predict this reference score. The models will be optimized to select the smallest subset of predictive food items (e.g., 10-15) while maximizing predictive accuracy (R²) in held-out validation sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, publicly available short dietary screener specifically for WFPB pattern adherence, validated in large, prospective cohorts. It will provide researchers and clinicians with a practical tool to rapidly assess holistic diet quality, facilitating the study of plant-based diets in relation to chronic disease risk and outcomes in diverse settings.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior screeners based on expert opinion or nutrient sums, this tool is empirically derived from population-level dietary data using pattern-recognition algorithms. It moves beyond simple checklists to capture the synergistic combinations of foods that characterize a high-quality WFPB diet.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Dual-Axis Dietary Screener for Ultra-Processed and Nutrient-Poor Foods in Relation to Type 2 Diabetes Risk
proposals.motivation: Dietary guidelines have traditionally focused on nutrients, but the NOVA classification highlights the independent health risks of ultra-processed foods (UPFs). However, not all UPFs are nutritionally poor, and not all nutrient-poor foods are ultra-processed. A tool that integrates both dimensions is needed to provide more precise, actionable dietary guidance for chronic disease prevention, particularly for type 2 diabetes, a major public health burden.
proposals.hypothesis: A dietary screener that jointly classifies foods by level of industrial processing and adherence to nutrient profiling models will identify a subset of 'high-priority' foods whose consumption is more strongly associated with incident type 2 diabetes risk than classifications based on either axis alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a screener algorithm that first classifies foods using the NOVA system. Concurrently, foods will be scored using a nutrient profiling model (e.g., based on limits for saturated fat, added sugar, sodium, and fiber). A composite priority score will be generated, with the highest scores assigned to foods that are both ultra-processed (NOVA group 4) and fail nutrient limits. We will validate this screener by applying it to repeated food frequency questionnaires from the Nurses' Health Studies and Health Professionals Follow-up Study, examining associations between the priority score and incident type 2 diabetes over follow-up, adjusting for physical activity and other confounders.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, practical tool for clinicians and public health practitioners to identify the most harmful dietary components efficiently. It will provide empirical evidence on whether the combination of processing and nutrient dimensions improves risk prediction, thereby refining dietary advice for diabetes prevention beyond current single-metric approaches.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by our group and others has examined processing or nutrients separately, this study innovatively combines them into a single, actionable metric. The adversarial integration addresses critiques that the UPF concept is too broad, by pinpointing the specific ultra-processed foods that are also nutritionally deficient.

proposals.title: Development and Prospective Validation of a Food Processing-Based Dietary Screener for Risk of Type 2 Diabetes and Obesity-Related Cancers
proposals.motivation: Current dietary guidelines and screeners often focus on isolated nutrients, which can be complex for individuals and may miss the synergistic effects of overall food quality. The NOVA classification system, which categorizes foods by degree of industrial processing, has emerged as a promising holistic metric, with pre-2026 prospective studies linking ultra-processed food (UPF) intake to higher risks of type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and certain cancers. A simple, validated screener based on this framework is needed for efficient use in large-scale epidemiology and clinical practice.
proposals.hypothesis: A brief dietary screener that quantifies habitual intake from higher NOVA processing groups (especially ultra-processed foods) will be strongly associated with future risk of type 2 diabetes and obesity-related cancers (e.g., pancreatic, colorectal), and this association will be independent of traditional nutrient-based diet quality scores.
proposals.method: We will develop a 15-20 item food frequency questionnaire (FFQ) module where participants report consumption frequency of key indicator foods from each NOVA group. The screener will be validated for reproducibility and relative validity against detailed FFQs and 24-hour recalls in sub-cohorts of the Nurses' Health Studies and Health Professionals Follow-up Study. We will then apply the screener to calculate a cumulative 'processing score' and use Cox proportional hazards models to estimate hazard ratios for incident type 2 diabetes and cancers over follow-up, adjusting for age, physical activity, BMI, smoking, and total energy intake.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, practical tool for rapidly assessing a dimension of diet quality linked to disease risk in large populations. It will advance the field by testing whether a processing-based metric offers predictive value beyond nutrient-focused models, potentially simplifying public health messaging. The findings could directly inform the design of preventive dietary interventions.
proposals.novelty: This proposal innovatively adapts the logic of hazard classification systems (like IARC's) to food screening, moving from constituent-based to processing-based risk assessment. While UPF-disease links are established, creating and validating a dedicated screener for this purpose, and testing its independent predictive utility, is a novel translational step.

proposals.title: Dietary Supplement Use as a Positive Modifier of Diet Quality Scores and Risk of Type 2 Diabetes and Cancer in the Nurses' Health Studies and Health Professionals Follow-up Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 diet quality indices like the Alternate Healthy Eating Index (AHEI) are based solely on food intake, potentially misclassifying individuals who intentionally use supplements to correct specific nutrient deficiencies or meet recommended intakes. This omission may bias observed associations between diet quality and chronic disease risk, as supplement use is prevalent and often targeted towards nutrients of public health concern (e.g., vitamin D, calcium).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating intentional supplementation for key shortfall nutrients (e.g., vitamin D, calcium, folate) as a positive component in a modified AHEI will strengthen the inverse associations between diet quality scores and the risk of incident type 2 diabetes and certain obesity-related cancers.
proposals.method: We will prospectively analyze pooled data from the Nurses' Health Studies I & II and the Health Professionals Follow-up Study. Supplement use (type, dose, frequency) is assessed biennially via validated questionnaires. We will create a modified AHEI where achieving the recommended intake for specific nutrients via diet *or* intentional supplementation yields the maximum score for that component. Using time-varying Cox proportional hazards models, we will compare hazard ratios for disease outcomes between the standard and modified AHEI, adjusting for age, physical activity, smoking, and other non-dietary factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more nuanced and clinically relevant tool for assessing overall nutritional status by formally integrating supplement use. It will clarify whether achieving nutrient goals through supplements confers a similar protective association with major chronic diseases as achieving them through food alone, informing both dietary assessment methodology and public health guidance.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Willett and others has treated supplements as separate exposures or confounders, this proposal innovates by directly embedding evidence-based supplement use into the core algorithm of a validated diet quality score, reconceptualizing it as a deliberate component of a high-quality dietary pattern.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Goal-Oriented Nutritional Sufficiency Score: Evaluating the Alignment of Combined Food and Supplement Intake with Personalized Cardiometabolic and Healthspan Targets
proposals.motivation: Current dietary quality indices, like the Alternate Healthy Eating Index, treat supplement use as a generic positive, failing to assess whether specific supplements effectively close nutrient gaps for an individual's health goals. With widespread supplement use and growing interest in personalized nutrition for managing conditions like diabetes and hypertension, a tool is needed to evaluate total nutrient intake (food + supplements) against goal-specific biomarker targets, moving beyond one-size-fits-all recommendations.
proposals.hypothesis: A dietary assessment algorithm that scores nutrient intake based on its efficacy in achieving personalized, physiology-based targets (e.g., HbA1c, blood pressure, recovery biomarkers) will more accurately predict health outcomes relevant to those goals than traditional dietary quality indices that consider supplements generically.
proposals.method: We will develop the GADSO algorithm using data from established cohorts (e.g., NHS, HPFS). For each participant, we will calculate total nutrient intake from repeated FFQs and detailed supplement inventories. The algorithm will define 'sufficiency' for each nutrient as the intake level associated, via pre-2026 published dose-response meta-analyses, with optimal values of goal-relevant biomarkers (e.g., magnesium for blood pressure). The primary score will be the proportion of goal-relevant nutrient targets met. We will validate the score by examining its association, using Cox models adjusted for physical activity and other confounders, with incident type 2 diabetes and hypertension over follow-up years, comparing its predictive performance to the AHEI.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, validated metric for nutritional epidemiology that shifts the focus from general diet quality to personalized nutrient sufficiency for specific health objectives. It will offer a framework to critically evaluate the real-world utility of supplement regimens within an individual's total dietary pattern, directly informing more precise public health and clinical guidance.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in dynamically defining dietary 'quality' based on personalized physiological targets rather than static population guidelines, and in algorithmically evaluating specific supplement use for its targeted efficacy rather than treating all supplementation as equally beneficial.

proposals.title: Test-Retest Reliability of the VEGANScreener Tool: A Psychometric Evaluation in a Cohort with Stable Dietary Patterns
proposals.motivation: Accurate dietary assessment is fundamental for large-scale epidemiological studies investigating associations between plant-based diets and chronic disease risk, such as type 2 diabetes and certain cancers. The VEGANScreener, a tool designed to rapidly identify adherence to a vegan diet, requires rigorous validation of its reliability to ensure its utility in prospective cohort research. Without established test-retest reliability, its application in long-term studies like the Nurses' Health Study or Health Professionals Follow-up Study is limited.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the VEGANScreener will demonstrate good to excellent test-retest reliability, as measured by intraclass correlation coefficients for total scores and Cohen's Kappa for individual items, when administered to individuals with a stable vegan dietary pattern over a short interval.
proposals.method: We will recruit 150 adults who self-report adhering to a vegan diet for at least one year. Participants will complete the VEGANScreener at baseline (T1) and again 3 weeks later (T2), a period chosen to minimize recall of initial answers while assuming dietary stability. Reliability will be quantified using a two-way mixed-effects model for absolute agreement to calculate Intraclass Correlation Coefficients (ICC) for the total screener score. For individual items, we will calculate Cohen's Kappa for categorical items and Pearson/Spearman correlations for continuous items. We will also assess internal consistency at both time points using Cronbach's alpha.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first formal psychometric evaluation of the VEGANScreener's temporal stability, a critical metric for its use in longitudinal research. It will establish benchmark reliability statistics and identify any items with poor reproducibility that may require refinement. This work will strengthen the methodological foundation for future studies examining vegan diets and disease outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While test-retest protocols are standard in clinical psychology, their application to validate novel dietary screening tools for specific dietary patterns like veganism is not routine. This study directly adapts these robust psychometric methods to address a gap in nutritional epidemiology tool validation.

proposals.title: Test-Retest Reliability and Reproducibility of the VEGANScreener in a Large Prospective Cohort: An Analysis from the Nurses' Health Study II
proposals.motivation: Accurate dietary assessment is critical for investigating associations between plant-based diet quality, chronic disease risk, and mortality. As plant-based diets gain prominence, validated tools like the VEGANScreener are needed for large-scale epidemiological research, but their reliability over time is unknown. Establishing the screener's reproducibility is a prerequisite for its use in prospective studies linking dietary patterns to outcomes like type 2 diabetes and cancer.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the VEGANScreener will demonstrate high test-retest reliability (intraclass correlation coefficient >0.70) and good agreement in categorical adherence classification over a one-year period in a stable adult population.
proposals.method: We will administer the VEGANScreener twice, approximately one year apart, to a randomly selected sub-cohort of 2,000 participants from the Nurses' Health Study II. Reliability will be assessed by calculating the intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC) for the continuous total score and weighted kappa statistics for quartiles of adherence. We will use the Bland-Altman method to evaluate limits of agreement and examine reliability within key subgroups defined by age, body mass index, and physical activity level.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide essential metrics on the temporal stability of a novel tool for assessing adherence to healthful plant-based diets. It will establish the VEGANScreener as a reliable instrument for longitudinal research, enabling more robust investigations into the associations between plant-based dietary patterns, cardiometabolic risk, and cancer incidence in future prospective analyses.
proposals.novelty: While test-retest reliability is standard for dietary questionnaires, it has not been formally established for the newly developed VEGANScreener, a tool specifically designed to capture the nuances of healthful and unhealthful plant-based food intake relevant to modern dietary patterns.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Digital Platform for Dynamic Nutritional Phenotyping and Personalized Cholesterol Metabolism Modeling in Prospective Cohort Studies
proposals.motivation: Dietary cholesterol and saturated fat intake are established modifiable risk factors for cardiometabolic diseases, yet individual metabolic responses vary widely. Current nutritional epidemiology relies on static intake assessments (e.g., food frequency questionnaires) which cannot capture the dynamic, time-dependent physiological response to dietary changes. A tool that translates dietary patterns into personalized, simulated metabolic outcomes would bridge a critical gap between population-level risk assessment and precision nutrition.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a validated dietary screener with a pharmacokinetic model of cholesterol absorption and turnover can generate accurate, personalized simulations of plasma cholesterol dynamics in response to specific dietary patterns, providing a novel digital phenotype more predictive of long-term health outcomes than intake data alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a mobile/web application integrating the VEGANScreener for dietary assessment. The backend will implement a validated, multi-compartment pharmacokinetic model of cholesterol metabolism, parameterized by individual inputs (e.g., dietary cholesterol, fiber, saturated fat). The model will simulate cholesterol pool dynamics over time using ordinary differential equations. We will validate the platform's predictions against serial plasma lipid measurements in a subset of participants from established cohorts like the Nurses' Health Study II, comparing simulated trajectories with observed biomarker data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel research tool that moves nutritional epidemiology from assessing static intake to modeling dynamic physiological response. It will enable the creation of a 'digital cholesterol phenotype' for large-scale studies, potentially improving risk stratification for diabetes and cardiovascular disease. The platform can also serve as a prototype for tailored dietary intervention planning in clinical settings.
proposals.novelty: While dietary screeners and cholesterol models exist independently, their fusion into a user-facing digital platform for real-time, personalized metabolic simulation is novel. This approach shifts the focus from correlation (intake vs. disease) to mechanistic, predictive modeling of an individual's physiological trajectory based on diet.

proposals.title: Calibrating Dietary Screeners to Individual Physiological Dynamics: An IRT-Pharmacokinetic Fusion for Personalized Risk Assessment
proposals.motivation: Current dietary assessment tools, like FFQs, are calibrated against population averages and have high measurement error, limiting their precision for individual-level risk prediction. This is a critical gap, as individual metabolic responses to nutrients like dietary cholesterol or sugar vary widely, influencing disease risk for conditions like diabetes and myocardial infarction. A method to dynamically personalize dietary assessment based on an individual's physiological response would significantly improve the accuracy of nutritional epidemiology.
proposals.hypothesis: That Item Response Theory (IRT) models, when calibrated against pharmacokinetic (PK) model estimates of an individual's nutrient pool dynamics derived from longitudinal biomarker data, can generate personalized dietary screener scores that more accurately predict subsequent metabolic shifts and disease risk than standard, population-calibrated tools.
proposals.method: We will use existing longitudinal cohort data (e.g., MVP, NHS/HPFS) with repeated measures of diet (via screeners), biomarkers (e.g., blood lipids, HbA1c), and health outcomes. For target nutrients (e.g., cholesterol, added sugar), we will fit Bayesian hierarchical PK models to individual biomarker trajectories to infer personal absorption/clearance parameters. These PK-derived 'physiological impact' estimates will serve as the criterion for calibrating IRT models (e.g., 2-parameter logistic models). The IRT item parameters (discrimination, difficulty) will be iteratively updated via Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) to maximize prediction of the PK state, creating a personalized scoring algorithm for the screener items.
proposals.contribution: This will produce a novel, validated framework for creating adaptive, personalized dietary assessment tools that move beyond static intake estimation. It will provide a more precise method to link dietary exposures to individual metabolic phenotypes, enhancing our ability to identify high-risk individuals in studies of diet, physical activity, and chronic diseases like type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in fusing psychometric measurement theory (IRT) with physiological pharmacokinetic modeling to personalize dietary assessment, creating a closed-loop calibration system where the 'gold standard' is not another dietary report but an individual's inferred internal metabolic state.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Machine Learning-Derived Ultra-Short Dietary Screener for Predicting Risk of Type 2 Diabetes and Cardiovascular Disease in Large Prospective Cohorts
proposals.motivation: Comprehensive dietary assessment tools like food frequency questionnaires are resource-intensive and impractical for large-scale clinical and public health screening. Existing short screeners are often based on expert opinion or simple regression, potentially missing key predictive dietary patterns for major chronic diseases. An empirically derived, highly predictive short screener could revolutionize risk assessment and personalized dietary counseling in diverse populations.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning algorithms applied to detailed baseline dietary data from large prospective cohort studies can identify a minimal set of food and beverage items that, when combined into a novel score, will robustly predict long-term incidence of type 2 diabetes and myocardial infarction, performing comparably to or better than longer established dietary indices.
proposals.method: We will utilize data from established cohorts like the Nurses' Health Studies and Health Professionals Follow-up Study, with detailed FFQ data and decades of follow-up for incident diabetes and MI. Using pre-2026 ML methods, we will train regularized regression models (LASSO, elastic net) and tree-based ensembles (random forests, gradient boosting) to select the most predictive dietary features for each outcome. The final model will be optimized to create a parsimonious scoring algorithm (e.g., 10-15 core items) that will be validated in external cohorts, including the Million Veteran Program, adjusting for key confounders like physical activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, empirically validated dietary risk screener that is brief yet highly predictive for two major cardiometabolic diseases. It will provide a practical tool for rapid risk stratification in clinical and public health settings. Furthermore, the ML-derived item selection will offer new insights into dietary combinations most strongly associated with disease risk, beyond traditional nutrient-based hypotheses.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been applied to dietary patterns, its use specifically to engineer an ultra-short, disease-predictive screener from prospective cohort data is novel. This approach moves beyond creating patterns for etiological insight to directly engineering a practical tool, leveraging ML's strength in feature selection from high-dimensional data to maximize predictive power with minimal items.

proposals.title: Expert-Constrained Pharmacokinetic Modeling of Dietary Cholesterol and Saturated Fat: A Novel Framework for Personalized Risk Assessment in Prospective Cohorts
proposals.motivation: Current dietary guidelines and risk models often treat nutrients in isolation, failing to capture the complex, dynamic interactions that determine metabolic outcomes like serum cholesterol levels and subsequent disease risk. Bridging the gap between qualitative expert consensus on dietary importance and quantitative systems biology is a critical unmet need in nutritional epidemiology. This limits the personalization of dietary advice for chronic disease prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: That a pharmacokinetic model of dietary cholesterol and saturated fat absorption and body pool kinetics, whose parameter space is constrained by expert-derived preference weights from conjoint analysis, will more accurately predict individual longitudinal changes in blood lipid profiles and associated disease risk than models informed by data alone.
proposals.method: We will first conduct a formal conjoint analysis survey with a panel of nutrition and lipidology experts to derive quantitative preference weights for dietary components (e.g., saturated fat type, dietary cholesterol, fiber) influencing cholesterol kinetics. These weights will be used to formulate Bayesian prior distributions to constrain the parameter estimation of a multi-compartment pharmacokinetic model. The model will be fitted to repeated dietary intake and biomarker data (e.g., blood lipids) from established cohorts like the Nurses' Health Studies and Health Professionals Follow-up Study, using Markov Chain Monte Carlo methods for inference.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, behaviorally-validated modeling framework that integrates clinical wisdom directly into systems biology. It will deliver a tool for generating personalized, dynamic predictions of metabolic response to diet. The methodology can be extended to other nutrient-disease pathways, advancing the field of precision nutrition.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of expert preference elicitation (conjoint analysis) with mechanistic pharmacokinetic modeling represents a non-obvious synergy, moving beyond purely data-driven or purely expert-opinion approaches. It formally encodes decades of clinical and epidemiological insight as constraints within a dynamic biological model.

proposals.title: Quantifying Expert Preferences for Dietary Pattern Components: A Conjoint Analysis Approach to Inform Public Health Guidelines
proposals.motivation: Public health dietary guidelines synthesize complex evidence on risk factors for major diseases like diabetes and cancer into simplified recommendations. However, the relative weight experts implicitly assign to different components (e.g., sugar-sweetened beverage intake vs. physical activity level vs. red meat consumption) when evaluating overall diet quality is not formally quantified. This lack of transparency can lead to inconsistencies in messaging and difficulty in prioritizing interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that conjoint analysis, a market research technique, can be successfully adapted to reveal and quantify the implicit weighting schemes that nutritional epidemiology experts use when evaluating the healthfulness of multi-component dietary patterns.
proposals.method: We will design a discrete choice experiment (DCE) using Sawtooth Software's CBC/HB (Choice-Based Conjoint/Hierarchical Bayes) system. A panel of 100-150 international nutritional epidemiologists (identified from cohorts like NHS, HPFS, and MVP) will be presented with 15-20 choice tasks. Each task will show 3-4 dietary profiles defined by attributes (e.g., SSB servings/day, whole grain intake, processed meat intake, physical activity level) with pre-2026 evidence-based levels. Participants will choose the 'healthiest' profile. Hierarchical Bayes estimation will derive individual-level part-worth utilities, quantifying the relative importance of each attribute in driving expert choice.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical, quantitative model of expert dietary preference weighting, moving beyond qualitative consensus. The resulting utility weights could inform the development of more transparent, evidence-weighted dietary indices. It will also identify areas of greatest expert consensus and divergence, highlighting priorities for future research synthesis.
proposals.novelty: While conjoint analysis is standard in consumer science, its application to deconstruct the decision-making of scientific experts in nutrition is novel. This methodologically bridges market research and nutritional epidemiology to make implicit expert judgments explicit and analyzable.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Dietary Patterns, Physical Activity, and Risk of Type 2 Diabetes: A Time-Varying Analysis in the Nurses' Health Studies and Health Professionals Follow-Up Study
proposals.motivation: Epidemiological studies of diet and chronic disease risk, such as diabetes and cancer, have traditionally relied on baseline or infrequent dietary assessments, assuming intake is stable over decades. This approach misclassifies long-term exposure, as dietary patterns demonstrably change over time due to personal choices, health events, and public health guidelines, potentially biasing risk estimates. Incorporating these time-varying patterns is critical for improving the accuracy of predictive models and understanding true long-term risk.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling dietary intake as a time-varying exposure, accounting for longitudinal changes in food and beverage consumption, will significantly improve the prediction of incident type 2 diabetes risk compared to models using only baseline or simple cumulative average intake. We further hypothesize that the association between changes in specific dietary components (e.g., sugar-sweetened beverages) and diabetes risk will be modified by concurrent levels of physical activity.
proposals.method: We will utilize repeated, validated food-frequency questionnaire data collected every 2-4 years from the Nurses' Health Studies I & II and the Health Professionals Follow-Up Study. Time-varying dietary patterns will be derived using latent class trajectory modeling to identify distinct trajectories of key dietary factors (e.g., SSB, artificially sweetened beverage, and alcohol intake) over the follow-up period. These time-varying trajectory classes, along with repeated measures of physical activity, will be incorporated as time-dependent covariates in extended Cox proportional hazards models with time-varying coefficients to assess their dynamic association with incident type 2 diabetes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a methodological framework for integrating longitudinal dietary data into chronic disease risk models, moving beyond static exposure measures. It will yield more accurate estimates of the long-term risks associated with dietary patterns and their interaction with physical activity, directly informing the timing and targeting of dietary interventions for diabetes prevention.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Willett and others has established the use of cumulative updating, this proposal advances the field by applying sophisticated trajectory modeling to characterize *patterns of change* in diet over time, rather than just updating a single exposure value. The explicit integration of these dynamic dietary trajectories with concurrent physical activity data in a time-varying effects model represents a novel analytical approach to understanding lifestyle-disease dynamics.

proposals.title: National Sodium Consumption Surveillance: A Novel Model-Based Estimation from NHANES and the UK Biobank
proposals.motivation: Excessive sodium intake is a leading modifiable risk factor for hypertension and cardiovascular disease, yet accurate population-level surveillance remains a challenge. Pre-2026, large national health surveys like NHANES rely on self-reported dietary data, which are subject to substantial measurement error and underreporting, limiting the precision of public health monitoring and policy evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a validated predictive model, integrating objective biomarkers (e.g., spot urine sodium), demographic factors, health behaviors, and physical activity data, can be applied to large national cohorts to produce more accurate and reliable estimates of habitual sodium consumption at the population level than self-report alone.
proposals.method: We will apply and scale a pre-existing, validated prediction model for 24-hour urinary sodium excretion, developed from controlled feeding studies, to the US National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES) and the UK Biobank. The model uses a multivariable-adjusted algorithm incorporating spot urine sodium, creatinine, age, sex, BMI, and physical activity. We will implement the algorithm to generate individual-level predicted sodium intake, aggregate results to estimate population distributions and trends, and compare these estimates to those derived from traditional 24-hour dietary recalls within the same cohorts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable framework for national nutrition surveillance of sodium, generating more robust population estimates to inform public health policy. It will directly demonstrate the utility of integrating biomarker-calibrated intake models into existing large-scale health surveys, moving the field beyond the limitations of self-reported dietary assessment for critical nutrients.
proposals.novelty: While prediction models for sodium exist, their systematic application to transform major national health surveys into precise surveillance tools for population sodium consumption represents a novel and practical advance in nutritional epidemiology, bridging the gap between methodological research and public health implementation.

proposals.title: Cardiovascular Disease Risk and a Strictly Plant-Based High-Quality Diet Score: A Prospective Cohort Analysis in the Nurses' Health Studies and Health Professionals Follow-Up Study
proposals.motivation: Dietary guidelines often promote plant-based diets for cardiovascular health but typically include moderate amounts of animal products like fish and low-fat dairy. The specific cardiovascular benefit attributable to the complete exclusion of animal products, beyond the benefits of a generally healthy plant-based diet, remains unclear and is a subject of public and scientific debate. This study directly addresses whether the highest tier of cardiovascular protection requires any animal-derived foods.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a strictly plant-based high-quality diet score (HPDS), which excludes all fish and dairy, will be associated with a lower risk of major cardiovascular events (myocardial infarction, stroke, and cardiovascular mortality) compared to a standard HPDS that includes these animal products, after adjusting for total energy intake and physical activity.
proposals.method: We will prospectively analyze pooled data from the Nurses' Health Study I, II, and the Health Professionals Follow-Up Study. Using validated food-frequency questionnaires, we will calculate two dietary indices: 1) a standard HPDS and 2) a strictly plant-based HPDS where points for fish and dairy are reallocated to other plant-based components. Time-varying Cox proportional hazards models will be used to estimate hazard ratios for incident CVD events over follow-up years, adjusting for age, BMI, smoking, physical activity, and other non-dietary risk factors. Formal comparison of model fit and risk estimates between the two scores will test for superiority.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide high-level epidemiological evidence on the necessity of fish and dairy for optimal cardiovascular health within a plant-predominant dietary pattern. It will inform dietary guidelines by clarifying if recommendations should emphasize complete avoidance of these animal products for maximal benefit. The findings will also offer practical guidance for individuals adhering to or considering vegan diets for health reasons.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has compared plant-based to non-plant-based diets, this study uniquely pits two versions of a *high-quality* diet against each other, isolating the marginal effect of excluding fish and dairy—foods often considered beneficial. It moves beyond broad diet patterns to test a specific, contested dietary prescription.

proposals.title: Dynamic Adherence to Healthy Plant-Based Diets and Long-Term Cardiovascular Disease Risk: An Analysis of Repeated Dietary Assessments in the Nurses' Health Studies and Health Professionals Follow-Up Study
proposals.motivation: Prospective cohort studies often link baseline diet quality, such as adherence to a healthy plant-based diet score (hPDI), to cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk, assuming stable dietary patterns. This assumption is unrealistic as diets change over time due to personal, social, and public health factors. Understanding how longitudinal changes in diet quality influence CVD risk is critical for refining dietary guidelines and personalizing prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that improvements in hPDI adherence over time will be associated with a lower risk of incident CVD, while declines in adherence will be associated with a higher risk, independent of baseline diet quality and other lifestyle factors.
proposals.method: We will use repeated, validated food-frequency questionnaire data from the NHS, NHS II, and HPFS cohorts, calculating hPDI at each time point (e.g., every 4 years). We will employ time-varying Cox proportional hazards models, treating hPDI as a time-updated exposure. We will also model specific trajectories of hPDI change (e.g., stable high, improving, declining) using group-based trajectory modeling and assess their association with CVD risk. Models will be adjusted for time-varying confounders including physical activity, BMI, smoking, and alcohol intake.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide novel evidence on the cardiovascular benefits of improving diet quality in mid-to-late adulthood. It will move the field beyond static, baseline assessments to a dynamic understanding of diet-disease relationships, offering stronger causal inference and more actionable public health messages about the value of dietary improvement at any age.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Willett and others has established the hPDI at baseline as a risk predictor, this analysis uniquely focuses on intra-individual change in the score over decades of follow-up, directly testing the impact of dietary improvement or decline on hard clinical endpoints.

proposals.title: Dietary Quality of Plant-Based Diets and Risk of Type 2 Diabetes and Obesity: A Prospective Cohort Study of Nurses' Health Study and Health Professionals Follow-up Study Participants
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, public health messaging and nutritional epidemiology often emphasized the benefits of 'plant-based' diets, but this broad categorization fails to distinguish between healthy (whole grains, fruits, vegetables) and unhealthy (refined grains, sugar-sweetened beverages, sweets) plant foods. The potential for high intake of these unhealthy plant foods to negate the cardiometabolic benefits associated with plant-based diets remains a critical, unresolved question with direct implications for dietary guidelines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a plant-based diet high in refined grains, potatoes, sugar-sweetened beverages, and sweets/juices will be associated with a higher risk of type 2 diabetes and weight gain, and that this risk will attenuate or negate the inverse association observed between overall plant-based diet indices and these outcomes.
proposals.method: We will leverage repeated dietary data from the Nurses' Health Study (NHS, 1986-2014), NHS II (1991-2017), and the Health Professionals Follow-up Study (HPFS, 1986-2014). Using validated food frequency questionnaires, we will construct two plant-based diet indices (PDI): a healthful PDI (hPDI) emphasizing healthy plant foods, and an unhealthful PDI (uPDI) emphasizing unhealthy plant foods. Primary outcomes are incident type 2 diabetes (confirmed by validated supplementary questionnaires) and change in body mass index (BMI). We will use time-varying Cox proportional hazards models for diabetes and linear mixed models for BMI, adjusting for age, total energy intake, physical activity, smoking, and other non-dietary confounders. Stratified analyses will examine effect modification by physical activity level.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed, longitudinal evidence quantifying how the quality of plant-based diets modifies their association with diabetes risk and adiposity. It will move the field beyond a simple plant vs. animal framework to offer nuanced, actionable guidance on which specific plant foods to emphasize or limit for chronic disease prevention.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Willett and others has examined individual food groups (e.g., sugary drinks), this study uniquely applies a novel dietary pattern index (uPDI) specifically designed to isolate the detrimental effects of low-quality plant foods within the context of an overall plant-based diet, directly testing the 'negation' hypothesis.

proposals.title: Optimizing the Healthy Plant-Based Diet Score: A Machine Learning Approach to Derive Food Group Weights for Predicting Type 2 Diabetes and Cardiovascular Disease Risk
proposals.motivation: The Healthy Plant-Based Diet Index (hPDI) is a widely used tool in nutritional epidemiology, but it assigns equal weights to its constituent food groups, an assumption that may not reflect their true, heterogeneous contributions to disease risk. Given the complex, non-linear relationships between diet and chronic diseases like type 2 diabetes and myocardial infarction—central themes in Dr. Willett's prior work—a data-driven weighting scheme could significantly improve the index's predictive validity and clinical utility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Healthy Plant-Based Diet Score (HPDS) with food group weights derived via machine learning from large prospective cohort data will have superior predictive power for incident type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease compared to the standard, equally weighted hPDI.
proposals.method: We will use data from the Nurses' Health Studies I & II and the Health Professionals Follow-up Study. For the 22 predefined food groups, we will employ gradient-boosted decision tree models (specifically XGBoost) to learn non-linear associations with incident disease outcomes, adjusting for confounders like physical activity, BMI, and smoking. The relative importance (gain) of each food group feature from the trained model will be used to derive continuous weights. The predictive performance of the ML-weighted HPDS will be rigorously compared to the standard hPDI using time-dependent AUC and likelihood ratio tests in validation cohorts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide an empirically optimized, more precise tool for assessing plant-based diet quality in research and public health. It will identify which food groups are most strongly predictive of major chronic diseases, offering clearer dietary guidance. The methodology can be extended to other dietary patterns, moving the field beyond heuristic scoring systems.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been applied to dietary patterns, its use to directly re-weight and validate a specific, established *a priori* index like the hPDI is novel. This approach bridges traditional nutritional epidemiology, which relies on biological plausibility, with modern data science to refine a key tool.

proposals.title: Associations of the Heart-Protective Diet Score with Incident Stroke, Peripheral Artery Disease, and Atrial Fibrillation: A Prospective Cohort Analysis
proposals.motivation: Dietary patterns are established modifiable risk factors for overall cardiovascular disease (CVD), but evidence for specific CVD subtypes is less consistent. Understanding whether a heart-protective diet confers differential protection against stroke, peripheral artery disease (PAD), and atrial fibrillation (AF) is critical for refining dietary guidelines and targeted prevention strategies. This investigation addresses a significant gap, as prior studies of composite diet scores often focus on coronary heart disease or total CVD mortality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a higher Heart-Protective Diet Score (HPDS) will be inversely associated with the risk of incident stroke and PAD, but its association with incident AF will be weaker or null, reflecting potentially distinct etiologies for arrhythmic versus atherosclerotic and thromboembolic outcomes.
proposals.method: We will conduct prospective analyses within the Nurses' Health Study and Health Professionals Follow-Up Study cohorts. The HPDS will be calculated from validated food frequency questionnaires, integrating pre-2026 evidence on foods beneficial (e.g., fruits, whole grains, nuts) and detrimental (e.g., red meat, sugary beverages) for heart health. Using time-varying Cox proportional hazards models, we will estimate hazard ratios for incident stroke (ischemic/hemorrhagic), PAD (clinically diagnosed or revascularization), and AF (confirmed by ECG), adjusting for age, BMI, physical activity, smoking, alcohol intake, and other non-dietary confounders. Stratified analyses will assess effect modification by sex and baseline hypertension status.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide novel, detailed evidence on how an integrated dietary pattern relates to three major CVD subtypes, informing more precise public health messages. It will clarify whether dietary recommendations for coronary disease prevention are equally applicable to stroke, PAD, and AF. The findings could guide the development of subtype-specific dietary adjuncts for high-risk populations.
proposals.novelty: While diet and total CVD are well-studied, a simultaneous, head-to-head prospective evaluation of a single heart-protective diet score against these three distinct, non-coronary CVD endpoints within large, detailed cohorts is novel. It moves beyond composite endpoints to elucidate potential mechanistic differences.

proposals.title: Mediation Analysis of the Healthy Plant-Based Diet Score and Cardiovascular Disease Risk: The Role of Inflammatory, Lipid, and Hemodynamic Biomarkers in the Nurses' Health Studies and Health Professionals Follow-Up Study
proposals.motivation: While prospective cohort studies, including our own, have established that higher adherence to a healthy plant-based diet (hPDI) is associated with a lower risk of cardiovascular disease (CVD), the specific biological pathways mediating this protective effect remain incompletely quantified. Understanding the relative contribution of key intermediate biomarkers, such as systemic inflammation, blood lipids, and blood pressure, is critical for refining dietary guidelines and developing targeted interventions. This investigation leverages decades of detailed, repeated dietary and biomarker data from our large, well-characterized cohorts to address this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the inverse association between a healthy plant-based diet score (hPDI) and incident CVD is significantly mediated through improvements in a panel of biomarkers, specifically reductions in systemic inflammation (hs-CRP, IL-6), favorable changes in lipid profiles (LDL-C, HDL-C, triglycerides), and lower blood pressure, with inflammation being a primary pathway.
proposals.method: We will use data from the Nurses' Health Study I & II and the Health Professionals Follow-Up Study, with repeated food-frequency questionnaires to calculate hPDI and confirmed CVD endpoints. Using a causal mediation analysis framework within Cox proportional hazards models, we will quantify the proportion of the total hPDI-CVD association mediated by each biomarker pathway. Analyses will treat biomarkers as time-varying mediators, account for confounding, and use bootstrapping to estimate 95% confidence intervals for the natural indirect effects. Sensitivity analyses will assess robustness to unmeasured mediator-outcome confounding.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, quantitative decomposition of the biological pathways linking a healthy plant-based diet to reduced CVD risk in large, long-term US cohorts. It will identify which biomarker pathways (e.g., inflammation vs. lipids) contribute most substantially to the diet's cardioprotective effect. The findings will offer mechanistic evidence to strengthen public health recommendations and inform the design of dietary interventions aimed at modulating specific biological risk factors.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has linked plant-based diets to individual biomarkers, this study employs advanced causal mediation methods on longitudinal data to formally partition and compare the contributions of multiple, concurrently acting biological pathways, moving beyond simple association to formal mediation testing within the established hPDI-CVD relationship.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Simplified Healthy Plant-Based Diet Score (sHPDS) for Type 2 Diabetes Risk Assessment in Primary Care
proposals.motivation: The 22-food-group Healthy Plant-Based Diet Index (hPDI) is a robust predictor of chronic disease risk, including type 2 diabetes and certain cancers, but its complexity limits its use in time-constrained primary care settings. A practical, validated tool is urgently needed to facilitate rapid dietary counseling and risk communication. Simplifying this evidence-based score could bridge the gap between epidemiological research and clinical practice, enabling actionable patient feedback.
proposals.hypothesis: A simplified Healthy Plant-Based Diet Score (sHPDS), derived from a reduced set of key food groups, will demonstrate strong correlation with the full hPDI and will be inversely associated with incident type 2 diabetes risk in large prospective cohorts.
proposals.method: Using data from the Nurses' Health Studies and Health Professionals Follow-up Study, we will apply stepwise regression and machine learning (LASSO regression) to identify the minimal set of food groups that best predict the full 22-group hPDI. The performance of the resulting sHPDS (e.g., 8-12 food groups) will be validated for predicting incident type 2 diabetes over follow-up years using Cox proportional hazards models, adjusting for age, physical activity, and other non-dietary risk factors. The sHPDS will be formatted into a one-page clinician and patient tool.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a clinically feasible dietary assessment tool directly derived from a major epidemiological index. It will provide validated, quantitative evidence for a streamlined dietary counseling protocol in primary care. The tool will enable clinicians to efficiently assess diet quality and communicate personalized diabetes risk based on pre-2026 evidence.
proposals.novelty: While shorter diet scores exist, this proposal uniquely creates a simplified instrument by directly distilling a complex, validated research index specifically for clinical application. The method leverages advanced variable selection on longitudinal data to preserve predictive validity for hard endpoints like diabetes, moving beyond mere correlation with the original score.

proposals.title: Network Analysis of Dietary Pattern Synergies and Their Association with Cardiovascular Disease Risk in the Nurses' Health Study and Health Professionals Follow-up Study
proposals.motivation: Traditional nutritional epidemiology often examines single nutrients or foods, but people eat complex combinations. This approach may miss critical synergistic or antagonistic interactions between food groups that collectively influence cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk. Applying network medicine, a successful paradigm in systems biology for complex diseases, offers a novel framework to model these dietary interactions and their systemic effects on health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dietary patterns can be modeled as interaction networks where specific food groups act as central nodes (hubs), and that the structure and connectivity of these networks, more than individual component intake, will be significantly associated with long-term CVD risk.
proposals.method: We will construct dietary networks using food frequency questionnaire data from the NHS, NHS II, and HPFS cohorts over multiple decades. Nodes will represent food groups, and edges will be weighted correlations (partial correlations) between their intake, adjusted for total energy. We will use community detection algorithms (e.g., Louvain method) to identify dietary modules and calculate network metrics (degree, betweenness centrality) for key food hubs. These network features will be linked to incident CVD events in Cox proportional hazards models, adjusted for standard confounders including physical activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, systems-level characterization of dietary patterns, moving beyond indices like the Mediterranean diet score. It will identify critical interactive components within diets that influence CVD, offering more precise targets for dietary guidelines. The methodology will establish a proof-of-concept for applying network medicine to nutritional epidemiology.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis is established in genomics, its application to model the intrinsic structure of dietary intake patterns and their relationship to chronic disease risk is novel. This moves beyond using networks to merely visualize dietary data to formally testing network properties as epidemiological risk factors.

proposals.title: A Reinforcement Learning Approach to Personalize Dietary Recommendations for Improving Adherence to the Healthy Plant-Based Diet Score (HPDS) in a Large Prospective Cohort
proposals.motivation: Adherence to healthy plant-based dietary patterns, such as the HPDS, is strongly associated with reduced risk of major chronic diseases, including type 2 diabetes and certain cancers. However, long-term adherence in free-living populations remains a major challenge due to the static nature of traditional dietary guidelines. Digital health tools offer a promising avenue for delivering dynamic, personalized feedback, but their application to dietary pattern adherence using advanced algorithms is nascent.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a reinforcement learning (RL) algorithm, trained on longitudinal dietary and health data, can generate personalized, adaptive dietary suggestions that will significantly improve adherence to the HPDS over time compared to static recommendations.
proposals.method: We will use data from the Nurses' Health Study II and Health Professionals Follow-up Study, leveraging repeated food frequency questionnaires, anthropometric data, and physical activity assessments. We will frame the problem as a Markov Decision Process where the state is defined by an individual's recent diet, activity, and health metrics, actions are specific dietary substitutions (e.g., replacing red meat with legumes), and the reward is the change in HPDS and key biomarkers (e.g., HbA1c). We will implement and compare model-free RL algorithms, specifically a contextual bandit approach for initial simplicity and a Deep Q-Network (DQN) for more complex, long-term optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept for using RL to personalize dietary guidance for chronic disease prevention at scale. It will yield a novel algorithmic framework that can adapt to individual preferences and responses, potentially increasing the effectiveness of public health nutrition interventions. The findings will contribute to the methodological integration of machine learning into nutritional epidemiology.
proposals.novelty: While RL has been explored for clinical decision support in areas like diabetes medication dosing, its application to optimizing adherence to a holistic dietary pattern score, using rich prospective cohort data, is a novel and non-obvious extension of Willett's prior work on diet-disease risk and behavioral factors like physical activity.

proposals.title: Mendelian Randomization Analysis of the Healthy Plant-Based Diet Score and Coronary Artery Disease Risk: A Multi-Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Observational studies consistently link healthier dietary patterns to lower cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk, but residual confounding and measurement error limit causal inference. The Healthy Plant-Based Diet Index (hPDI) is a validated score associated with lower diabetes and some cancer risks, but its causal relationship with coronary artery disease (CAD) remains uncertain. Mendelian Randomization (MR) can strengthen causal evidence by using genetic variants as instrumental variables, but it has rarely been applied to complex, multi-component diet scores like the hPDI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a genetically predicted higher Healthy Plant-Based Diet Score (hPDI) is causally associated with a lower risk of incident coronary artery disease. Furthermore, we hypothesize that this relationship is partially mediated by improvements in cardiometabolic risk factors, including body mass index, blood lipids, and type 2 diabetes status.
proposals.method: We will construct a polygenic score (PGS) for the hPDI using genetic variants associated with dietary components (e.g., whole grains, fruits, vegetables, legumes) identified in large-scale genome-wide association studies (GWAS) like UK Biobank. This PGS will serve as the instrumental variable. We will apply two-sample MR analyses, using summary statistics from the hPDI PGS derivation and from large CAD consortia (e.g., CARDIoGRAMplusC4D). We will employ inverse-variance weighted MR as the primary method, supplemented by MR-Egger and weighted median estimators to assess pleiotropy. Mediation analysis will quantify the proportion of the effect mediated through established metabolic pathways.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the strongest pre-2026 genetic evidence for a causal effect of a healthful plant-based diet on CAD, directly informing dietary guidelines. It will demonstrate the novel application of MR to a holistic diet score, moving beyond single nutrients or foods. The mediation analysis will elucidate the biological pathways through which the diet exerts its protective effect.
proposals.novelty: While MR has been used for single dietary factors, applying it to a pre-defined, holistic diet score like the hPDI is a novel methodological advance. Furthermore, integrating mediation analysis within the MR framework to deconstruct the causal pathway for a complex exposure is a non-obvious extension of existing methods.

proposals.title: Objective Adherence to a Heart-Protective Diet Using Continuous Glucose Monitoring: Associations with Cardiometabolic Risk in the Nurses' Health Studies
proposals.motivation: Dietary assessment in large cohorts relies on self-report, which is subject to measurement error and recall bias, potentially obscuring true diet-disease relationships. Continuous glucose monitors (CGMs) provide high-resolution, objective data on glycemic response, a key physiological mediator of diet quality. Using CGM-derived metrics as objective proxies for adherence to a heart-protective diet could substantially improve risk estimation for cardiometabolic diseases like type 2 diabetes and coronary heart disease.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CGM-derived metrics (e.g., time-in-range, glycemic variability) will be strongly correlated with self-reported adherence to a heart-protective diet (e.g., Alternate Mediterranean Diet Score) and that these objective metrics will be more strongly associated with incident type 2 diabetes and myocardial infarction than self-reported diet scores.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing CGM data from sub-studies within the Nurses' Health Studies (NHS and NHS II). For each participant, we will calculate standard CGM metrics (mean glucose, glycemic variability, time-in-range 70-140 mg/dL) over a 10-14 day period. We will use Spearman correlations and multivariable linear regression to assess the cross-sectional relationship between these metrics and concurrent aMED scores. Subsequently, we will use Cox proportional hazards models to compare the associations of the aMED score and the CGM-derived metrics (modeled as continuous and in quintiles) with risk of incident type 2 diabetes and myocardial infarction, adjusting for age, BMI, physical activity, and other non-dietary confounders.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, objective validation of a key dietary pattern using physiologically anchored sensor data. It will demonstrate the utility of wearable devices for refining nutritional epidemiology and could establish CGM metrics as a new, objective intermediate endpoint for dietary intervention trials. The findings will strengthen the evidence linking diet quality to cardiometabolic health by reducing measurement error.
proposals.novelty: While CGMs are used in diabetes management, their application as an objective tool to validate and enhance dietary adherence assessment in a large, prospective cohort setting is novel. This moves beyond using self-report alone to triangulate diet-disease associations with physiological data.

proposals.title: Synergistic Effects of Dietary Fiber and Polyphenols on Type 2 Diabetes Risk: A Deconstruction of the Healthy Plant-Based Diet Score in the NHS, NHS II, and HPFS Cohorts
proposals.motivation: The Healthy Plant-Based Diet Index (hPDI) robustly predicts lower risk of type 2 diabetes and other chronic diseases, but its protective mechanisms are attributed to broad food groups. This obscures the specific, potentially interactive roles of bioactive micronutrients within those foods. Understanding these nutrient-level interactions is critical for moving beyond food-based recommendations to more precise, mechanistic dietary guidance for chronic disease prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the protective association of a high hPDI with type 2 diabetes is driven not merely by the additive effects of its components, but by synergistic interactions between specific nutrients, particularly dietary fiber and polyphenols, which together modulate metabolic pathways more effectively than either nutrient alone.
proposals.method: We will leverage long-term dietary data from the Nurses' Health Studies (NHS, NHS II) and the Health Professionals Follow-up Study (HPFS). Using repeated food-frequency questionnaires, we will calculate nutrient intakes, with a focus on fiber and polyphenol subclasses (e.g., flavonoids, phenolic acids). We will employ multivariable-adjusted Cox proportional hazards models to assess individual and joint associations. To test for synergy, we will model interaction terms on both additive and multiplicative scales and use compositional data analysis techniques to deconstruct the hPDI into its nutrient-level constituents, examining their joint effects beyond the food-group score.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed nutrient-level deconstruction of a validated plant-based diet score, identifying specific synergistic interactions that drive its health benefits. The findings will advance nutritional epidemiology from food-based to nutrient-interaction frameworks, offering novel biological insights and supporting more nuanced dietary recommendations for diabetes prevention.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links the hPDI to disease risk, no study has systematically dissected it to test for synergistic nutrient interactions within its structure. This approach moves beyond analyzing single nutrients or food groups to model their interactive effects, a critical step toward understanding diet as a complex system.

proposals.title: Modeling the Population-Level Cardiovascular Disease Impact of National Policies to Promote Healthy Plant-Based Diet Score-Aligned Food Production and Availability
proposals.motivation: Cardiovascular disease (CVD) remains the leading cause of mortality globally, with diet as a primary modifiable risk factor. While cohort studies like the Nurses' Health Study have established individual-level associations between diet and CVD risk, there is a critical gap in translating these findings into actionable national policy. Pre-2026, there is limited modeling of how systemic shifts in food production and availability, aligned with dietary patterns like the Healthy Plant-Based Diet Score (HPDS), could impact population-wide CVD burden.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that national food policies designed to increase the production and availability of HPDS-aligned foods (e.g., whole grains, fruits, nuts, legumes) will lead to a significant reduction in the population-level incidence of myocardial infarction and stroke over a 10-year period.
proposals.method: We will develop a comparative risk assessment model using pre-2026 data. The model will integrate: 1) nationally representative dietary intake data (e.g., NHANES) to establish baseline consumption, 2) relative risk estimates for CVD endpoints from pooled analyses of prospective cohorts (e.g., HPDS-CVD meta-analyses), 3) policy scenarios defining specific shifts in food availability (e.g., 20% increase in whole grain availability) and corresponding changes in population intake, and 4) demographic and CVD incidence data from sources like the Global Burden of Disease study. We will use Monte Carlo simulation to propagate uncertainty in all input parameters and estimate preventable CVD cases and deaths.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, policy-actionable estimates of how structural changes in the U.S. food system, guided by the HPDS, can reduce CVD mortality. It will move the field from individual dietary advice to evidence-based national food policy planning. The model will serve as a transparent tool for policymakers to evaluate the potential health impact of agricultural and economic interventions.
proposals.novelty: While dietary modeling exists, this approach uniquely integrates the HPDS—a comprehensive diet quality metric—with national agricultural and economic policy levers, moving beyond taxation or single-nutrient strategies to model a holistic, food-based systemic shift.

proposals.title: Inferring Adherence to the Healthy Plant-Based Diet Score from Electronic Health Records of Cardiovascular Disease Patients Using Machine Learning
proposals.motivation: The Healthy Plant-Based Diet Score (HPDS) is strongly associated with reduced risk of cardiovascular disease (CVD), diabetes, and cancer in prospective cohorts. However, assessing long-term dietary patterns in large, real-world clinical populations is resource-intensive and often unavailable. Electronic Health Records (EHRs) contain rich, longitudinal data on diagnoses, medications, and lab values that may serve as proxies for dietary habits, offering a scalable way to study diet-disease relationships in diverse patient populations.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning models can accurately infer an individual's likely adherence to an HPDS pattern using routinely collected EHR data, with the inferred score being predictive of subsequent cardiometabolic outcomes among patients with established CVD.
proposals.method: We will use data from large, longitudinal cohorts like the Nurses' Health Studies and Health Professionals Follow-up Study, where HPDS is known, linked to Medicare claims or other EHR data. We will train gradient-boosted tree models (XGBoost) and neural networks to predict the continuous HPDS from features including diagnosis codes (ICD-9/10), medication prescriptions (e.g., for diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidemia), laboratory results (e.g., lipids, HbA1c), and anthropometric measures over a multi-year period. Model performance will be validated in held-out cohorts and tested for generalizability in independent EHR datasets like the Million Veteran Program.
proposals.contribution: This work will develop and validate a novel method for large-scale, retrospective dietary pattern assessment in clinical populations, enabling diet-outcome research where direct dietary data is absent. It will provide insights into the clinical signatures of plant-based diet adherence and create a tool to study the association of inferred diet with disease progression and mortality in CVD patients.
proposals.novelty: While machine learning is used to predict disease from risk factors, applying it to solve the inverse problem—inferring a protective dietary pattern from disease status and its clinical correlates—is a novel approach to nutritional epidemiology. This moves beyond using EHRs for phenotyping disease to phenotyping health behaviors.

proposals.title: Associations of the Healthy Plant-Based Diet Score (HPDS) with Incident Type 2 Diabetes, Site-Specific Cancers, and All-Cause Mortality: A Pooled Analysis of Prospective Cohort Studies
proposals.motivation: Dietary patterns emphasizing plant-based foods are associated with lower risk of major chronic diseases, but the specific health impact of the *quality* of plant-based diets remains incompletely characterized for multiple outcomes. The Healthy Plant-Based Diet Score (HPDS), which distinguishes healthy from less healthy plant foods, has been linked to cardiometabolic outcomes, but its associations with incident cancer and all-cause mortality require further large-scale, prospective investigation to inform comprehensive dietary guidelines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a higher HPDS, indicating greater consumption of healthy plant foods and lower intake of unhealthy plant and animal foods, will be associated with a lower risk of incident type 2 diabetes, several site-specific cancers (e.g., colorectal, breast), and all-cause mortality in a dose-response manner.
proposals.method: We will apply the established HPDS framework to harmonized individual-level data from at least three large prospective cohorts (e.g., NHS, NHS II, HPFS). Dietary intake will be assessed via validated food-frequency questionnaires and scored to create HPDS. We will use time-varying Cox proportional hazards models to estimate hazard ratios (HRs) and 95% confidence intervals for each outcome, adjusting for confounders including age, BMI, physical activity, smoking, and total energy intake. Analyses will be stratified by cohort and sex, with tests for linear trend across HPDS quintiles.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a unified, quantitative assessment of how a high-quality plant-based diet pattern relates to three critical public health endpoints. It will clarify whether the benefits of the HPDS extend beyond cardiometabolic disease to cancer prevention and longevity, offering evidence to refine dietary recommendations for chronic disease prevention.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has linked plant-based diets to individual diseases, this analysis simultaneously evaluates the HPDS—a metric capturing diet quality within a plant-based framework—across diabetes, cancer, and mortality in a single, large-scale, pooled prospective design, allowing for direct comparison of association strengths.

proposals.title: Joint Associations of Dietary Cholesterol and Saturated Fat Intake with Myocardial Infarction Risk: An Analysis of the Million Veteran Program
proposals.motivation: Dietary cholesterol and saturated fat are often consumed together in foods like red meat and eggs, yet their independent and joint effects on cardiovascular disease risk remain debated. Prior analyses, including Willett's 2024 study in the Million Veteran Program (MVP), have examined these nutrients separately, but their biological pathways for raising LDL cholesterol are interrelated. Understanding their interaction is critical for refining dietary guidelines and providing more nuanced public health advice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the association between dietary cholesterol and myocardial infarction (MI) risk is modified by the level of saturated fat intake, with the highest risk observed in individuals with high intake of both nutrients.
proposals.method: We will leverage the existing MVP cohort with detailed dietary data (via food frequency questionnaires) and adjudicated MI outcomes. Using multivariable Cox proportional hazards models, we will assess the main effects and interaction between energy-adjusted dietary cholesterol and saturated fat (both modeled as continuous and categorical variables). We will test for statistical interaction by including a product term in the models and stratifying analyses by median saturated fat intake, adjusting for age, sex, ancestry, physical activity, smoking, alcohol, and other dietary factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, prospective evidence on the interaction between dietary cholesterol and saturated fat on MI risk. The findings will clarify whether dietary recommendations should consider these nutrients jointly and help identify high-risk dietary patterns for targeted intervention.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have examined these nutrients in isolation, this analysis directly tests their statistical interaction within a single, massive cohort, moving beyond independent associations to model their real-world co-consumption.

proposals.title: Strengthening Causal Inference in Nutritional Epidemiology: A Formal Application of Econometric Instrumental Variable Validation to Mendelian Randomization Studies of Dietary Cholesterol and Cardiovascular Disease
proposals.motivation: Observational studies on diet and health, like those on dietary cholesterol and myocardial infarction, are plagued by confounding and reverse causality. Mendelian randomization (MR) uses genetic variants as instruments to infer causality, but its validity critically depends on assumptions (exclusion restriction, no pleiotropy) that are often violated or poorly tested. Pre-2026, MR methods are evolving, but the field lacks a formal, rigorous framework for instrument validation, a cornerstone of causal inference in econometrics.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying the formal statistical tests and validation framework for instrumental variables (IV) developed in econometrics—including tests for instrument strength, exogeneity, and the exclusion restriction—to MR studies will significantly improve the detection and mitigation of pleiotropic bias, leading to more robust and defensible causal estimates of dietary cholesterol's impact on cardiovascular disease.
proposals.method: We will re-analyze data from large-scale cohorts (e.g., Million Veteran Program, NHS/HPFS) examining dietary cholesterol and CVD. Using genetic variants associated with dietary cholesterol intake as instruments, we will systematically apply econometric IV diagnostics: the F-statistic for weak instrument testing, the Sargan-Hansen J-test for overidentifying restrictions to detect invalid instruments (pleiotropy), and Durbin-Wu-Hausman tests for endogeneity. We will compare causal estimates from standard MR methods (e.g., inverse-variance weighted) with those from econometric IV estimators (e.g., two-stage least squares) that formally incorporate these validation steps.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, transferable framework for strengthening causal claims in nutritional epidemiology. It will yield a methodological paper and a practical guide for applying econometric IV rigor to MR, directly improving the reliability of causal inferences for major dietary risk factors like cholesterol, sugar-sweetened beverages, and alcohol in relation to chronic diseases.
