proposals.novelty: While MR is conceptually an IV method, its practice has not fully integrated the formal, battery-based validation protocols standard in econometrics for decades. This proposal explicitly bridges that gap, treating genetic instruments with the same statistical rigor applied to policy instruments, which is a non-obvious and systematic advancement for the field.

proposals.title: A Dual-Isotope Tracer and Metabolomics Study to Identify and Validate Biomarkers of Dietary Cholesterol Intake Independent of Endogenous Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Epidemiological studies on dietary cholesterol and cardiometabolic disease risk, such as our work in the Million Veteran Program, are fundamentally limited by the inaccuracy of self-reported intake and the confounding influence of highly variable endogenous cholesterol synthesis. This methodological gap prevents a clear understanding of the true health effects of dietary cholesterol, a critical public health question. A validated biomarker of dietary cholesterol intake is therefore essential to advance nutritional epidemiology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by using a controlled feeding study with isotopically labeled cholesterol, we can identify a distinct metabolomic signature in plasma that is directly attributable to dietary cholesterol intake, and that this signature can be mathematically separated from the metabolic background of endogenous synthesis measured by a second tracer.
proposals.method: We will conduct a randomized, controlled feeding study with 150 participants. Participants will consume meals containing precisely dosed cholesterol labeled with deuterium (d7-cholesterol) to trace dietary intake, while endogenous synthesis will be simultaneously measured via an oral dose of 13C-acetate. Serial blood samples will undergo high-throughput untargeted metabolomic profiling (LC-MS/MS). We will use supervised machine learning models, specifically L1-penalized (LASSO) regression and random forests, to identify metabolite patterns whose concentrations correlate with the enrichment of the dietary tracer. These models will be trained to predict dietary tracer flux while adjusting for the endogenous synthesis rate derived from the second tracer.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first validated panel of metabolomic biomarkers that specifically reflect dietary cholesterol intake, correcting for individual metabolic variation. This panel will provide an objective tool for future large-scale cohort studies to accurately assess the association of dietary cholesterol with incident diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and cancer risk, moving the field beyond reliance on food frequency questionnaires.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of two concurrent isotopic tracers within a metabolomics framework creates an internal control experiment, allowing us to disentangle the dietary signal from endogenous metabolic noise for the first time. This adversarial design directly addresses the core confounding problem that has plagued nutritional research on cholesterol.

proposals.title: A Context-Embedded PBPK-Nutritional Model for Personalized Cardiovascular Risk Stratification: Integration of Dietary Cholesterol, Genetics, Microbiome, and Inflammation
proposals.motivation: While large cohort studies, including our work in the Million Veteran Program, have established links between dietary cholesterol intake and cardiovascular disease risk, substantial inter-individual variability in response exists. This variability is poorly captured by simple intake-outcome associations, limiting personalized prevention. A mechanistic, systems-level model that integrates known physiological modulators of cholesterol metabolism is needed to move from population-level risk to dynamic, individualized risk trajectories.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a physiologically based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) model for dietary cholesterol, when embedded with modules representing genetic (e.g., APOE variants), microbial (bile acid metabolism), hepatic (LDL receptor feedback), and systemic inflammatory contexts, will significantly improve the prediction of individual longitudinal lipoprotein profiles and cardiovascular event risk compared to models based on intake alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a modular PBPK model using ordinary differential equations to simulate cholesterol absorption, hepatic uptake, and plasma kinetics. Context modules will be parameterized using pre-2026 data: genetic risk scores from GWAS, microbiome-derived bile acid profiles from metagenomic studies, and inflammatory markers like hs-CRP. The model will be calibrated and validated against existing longitudinal data from cohorts with detailed dietary records (e.g., NHS, HPFS), serial biomarker panels, and adjudicated cardiovascular outcomes, using Bayesian hierarchical modeling for parameter estimation and machine learning (e.g., random forests) to weight context module contributions to final risk prediction.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, open-source simulation tool that quantifies how an individual's physiological context modulates the cardiovascular risk posed by dietary cholesterol. It will provide a novel framework for generating personalized, dynamic risk assessments and for testing mechanistic hypotheses about diet-disease pathways that are not directly observable in human trials.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in nutrition epidemiology for its explicit integration of a mechanistic PBPK structure with multiple, modifiable physiological context layers to personalize disease risk prediction, moving beyond the static, main-effect models that dominate the field pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Signal Detection Theory Model of Dietary Cholesterol Intake Recalibration Following Myocardial Infarction: Analysis from the Nurses' Health Study and Health Professionals Follow-up Study
proposals.motivation: Despite clear evidence linking dietary cholesterol to cardiovascular risk, adherence to post-MI dietary guidelines is highly variable and often unsustainable. Current research quantifies intake but fails to model the perceptual and behavioral adaptation triggered by the traumatic health event itself. Understanding this adaptation is crucial for designing interventions that support lasting, healthful dietary change in high-risk survivors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an MI acts as a salient signal that recalibrates a patient's internal decision threshold for consuming dietary cholesterol, shifting the trade-off between perceived dietary risk (false alarm) and dietary reward (missed detection), and that the magnitude of this threshold shift predicts long-term adherence patterns.
proposals.method: We will apply Signal Detection Theory (SDT) frameworks, analogous to those used in ecology to model predator-prey interactions, to longitudinal dietary data from the NHS and HPFS cohorts. For each participant, pre- and post-MI periods will be analyzed. The consumption of high-cholesterol foods will be treated as a decision under uncertainty. We will model the internal criterion (threshold) for consumption, calculating sensitivity (d') and criterion shift (c) using established SDT algorithms. The primary analysis will quantify the post-MI criterion shift and its association with subsequent adherence trajectories, physical activity, and diabetes risk, using mixed-effects models.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond simple intake measurement to provide a computational model of behavioral adaptation after a major health shock. It will quantify the perceptual trade-offs patients make, offering a novel framework to predict which survivors sustain dietary change and why. This can inform personalized behavioral counseling strategies that account for individual risk perception.
proposals.novelty: The application of a formal Signal Detection Theory framework from behavioral ecology to model dietary change after an MI is novel. It reframes dietary adherence not merely as a compliance issue but as a dynamic perceptual recalibration process, a perspective not previously applied in nutritional epidemiology.

proposals.title: Culturally Adapted Validation of the VEGANScreener and Longitudinal Assessment of Plant-Based Dietary Transitions in Post-Myocardial Infarction Patients Across Europe: The EURO-POST-MI Diet Study
proposals.motivation: Dietary modification towards plant-based patterns is a cornerstone of secondary prevention after myocardial infarction (MI), yet adherence remains poor. Existing studies, including our work on dietary cholesterol and MI risk, often rely on isolated nutrient metrics rather than holistic dietary patterns, and culturally adapted tools for monitoring such transitions across diverse European populations are lacking. This gap hinders effective, personalized nutritional counseling and support programs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a culturally adapted and validated VEGANScreener will reliably capture post-MI dietary transitions towards plant-based patterns across European countries, and that longitudinal changes in screener scores will be inversely associated with key cardiometabolic risk factors, with the strength of association modified by cultural, linguistic, and socioeconomic factors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of 5,000 recent post-MI patients recruited from cardiology clinics in five European countries (e.g., UK, Spain, Poland, Italy, Sweden). The core technical approach involves a multi-phase cultural adaptation and validation of the existing VEGANScreener using cognitive interviewing and confirmatory factor analysis. Dietary intake will be assessed at baseline, 6, 12, and 24 months using the adapted screener and validated country-specific food frequency questionnaires. We will employ linear mixed-effects models to analyze longitudinal changes in dietary patterns (screener score as primary outcome), and Cox proportional hazards models to assess associations between dietary change and composite cardiovascular endpoints, adjusting for confounders including physical activity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first culturally validated tool for monitoring plant-based dietary adherence in a high-risk clinical population across Europe. It will generate novel longitudinal data on the real-world facilitators and barriers to dietary change post-MI, directly informing the design of targeted, equitable secondary prevention programs. The findings will extend evidence beyond single nutrients to holistic dietary patterns in cardiometabolic health.
proposals.novelty: This work moves beyond assessing static intake of specific dietary components (e.g., cholesterol, sugar-sweetened beverages) to dynamically monitor the holistic adoption of a recommended dietary pattern using a novel, adapted instrument. It uniquely integrates rigorous cross-cultural tool validation with longitudinal epidemiology to address implementation gaps in dietary secondary prevention.

proposals.title: Red Meat Consumption and Risk of Type 2 Diabetes and Colorectal Cancer: A Matched Cohort Analysis Accounting for Overall Diet Quality and Socioeconomic Status in the Nurses' Health Studies and Health Professionals Follow-up Study
proposals.motivation: Observational studies consistently link red meat intake to increased risk of type 2 diabetes and colorectal cancer. However, red meat consumption is strongly correlated with other unhealthy lifestyle factors and lower socioeconomic status, which are themselves powerful risk factors. It remains unclear whether the observed associations are directly causal or substantially confounded by these correlated dietary and socioeconomic patterns.
proposals.hypothesis: After rigorous matching on overall diet quality (using the Alternate Healthy Eating Index-2010) and a composite socioeconomic status (SES) score, the magnitude of the association between red meat consumption and the risk of type 2 diabetes and colorectal cancer will be substantially attenuated compared to conventional multivariate-adjusted models.
proposals.method: We will use data from the long-running Nurses' Health Studies I & II and the Health Professionals Follow-up Study. Using a propensity score matching algorithm, participants in the highest quintile of red meat intake will be matched 1:1 to participants in the lowest quintile based on their AHEI-2010 score (within 2 points) and a composite SES score (incorporating education, household income, and census-tract data). After matching, we will use Cox proportional hazards models to estimate hazard ratios for incident type 2 diabetes and colorectal cancer, comparing the matched high- and low-intake groups, with further adjustment for physical activity, smoking, and alcohol intake.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a more robust estimate of the independent effect of red meat by minimizing residual confounding from the broader dietary pattern and socioeconomic context. It will directly inform dietary guidelines by clarifying whether recommendations should focus specifically on limiting red meat or on improving overall diet quality and addressing socioeconomic determinants of health.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies adjust for these factors as covariates, this study employs a rigorous matching design to explicitly create comparable groups that differ primarily in red meat intake, not in overall diet quality or SES—a method not yet widely applied to this specific question in large, prospective cohorts.

proposals.title: Non-linear dose-response relationships between sugar-sweetened beverage consumption, physical activity, and incident type 2 diabetes: A pooled analysis of prospective cohort studies
proposals.motivation: Public health guidelines often assume linear relationships between dietary exposures and disease risk, but this may be inaccurate. Identifying potential thresholds or non-linear effects is critical for refining dietary recommendations and targeting interventions. This is particularly relevant for sugar-sweetened beverages (SSBs), a major source of added sugars, and physical activity, a key modifiable factor for type 2 diabetes prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the associations of SSB consumption and physical activity with incident type 2 diabetes are non-linear, with risk increasing more sharply beyond a specific threshold of SSB intake and decreasing more steeply below a specific threshold of physical activity.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pooled analysis of individual-level data from at least five large prospective cohorts (e.g., NHS, HPFS, NHS II, ARIC, MESA) with repeated dietary and physical activity assessments. Using restricted cubic splines in Cox proportional hazards models, we will formally test for non-linearity in the dose-response relationships. We will model SSB intake (servings/day) and physical activity (MET-hours/week) as continuous exposures, adjusting for age, BMI, smoking, alcohol, and other dietary factors. Likelihood ratio tests will compare the spline models to linear models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide precise, quantitative evidence on the shape of the dose-response curves, identifying potential intake levels where risk accelerates or plateaus. The findings will directly inform the refinement of public health guidelines by suggesting specific quantitative limits for SSB consumption and targets for physical activity to prevent type 2 diabetes.
proposals.novelty: While prior work establishes associations, few large-scale pooled analyses have formally tested for non-linearity using advanced spline models specifically for the joint consideration of SSBs and physical activity. This moves beyond reporting relative risks for arbitrary intake categories to characterize the continuous risk function.

proposals.title: Real-Time Digital Phenotyping of Cognitive Function: A Prospective Cohort Study on the Association with Dietary Patterns and Risk of Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Subtle, acute fluctuations in cognitive performance may be sensitive early markers of metabolic dysregulation and long-term disease risk, but traditional episodic neuropsychological testing cannot capture these dynamics. The widespread adoption of smartphones enables high-frequency, ecologically valid cognitive assessment (digital phenotyping), which, when paired with detailed dietary data, can provide novel insights into the diet-cognition-disease pathway. This is critical for understanding modifiable risk factors for cognitive decline and diabetes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that higher frequency of sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) consumption and lower diet quality scores will be associated with greater intra-individual variability and slower reaction times on smartphone-based cognitive tasks, and that these digital cognitive phenotypes will mediate part of the association between diet and incident type 2 diabetes.
proposals.method: We will recruit 50,000 participants from ongoing cohorts (e.g., Nurses' Health Study II, Health Professionals Follow-up Study) to install a custom research app. The app will deploy brief, gamified cognitive tests (e.g., Stroop, n-back, simple reaction time) 2-3 times daily via random prompts, collecting millisecond-accurate performance data. Dietary intake will be assessed via validated semi-annual food frequency questionnaires and linked SSB consumption data. We will use mixed-effects models to analyze between-person and within-person associations, and causal mediation analysis to test the role of cognitive metrics. Algorithms will include change-point detection for identifying significant performance shifts.
proposals.contribution: This study will establish a novel methodology for capturing real-world cognitive variability as a digital biomarker. It will provide the first large-scale, prospective evidence linking dynamic cognitive performance to specific dietary patterns and subsequent diabetes risk, moving beyond static cognitive assessments. The findings could inform real-time dietary interventions for cognitive and metabolic health.
proposals.novelty: While digital phenotyping has been used in psychiatry, its application to nutritional epidemiology for high-frequency cognitive assessment in relation to diet and diabetes risk is novel. This approach moves beyond assessing long-term cognitive decline to capture acute, diet-related cognitive fluctuations as a potential mechanistic pathway.

proposals.title: Metabolomic Biomarkers of Red Meat Intake and Their Association with Incident Type 2 Diabetes and Colorectal Cancer Risk in the Nurses' Health Studies and Health Professionals Follow-Up Study
proposals.motivation: Epidemiological studies, including our own, have consistently linked high red meat intake to increased risk of type 2 diabetes and colorectal cancer. However, reliance on self-reported dietary data from food frequency questionnaires (FFQs) introduces measurement error and recall bias, potentially obscuring true risk estimates and underlying biological pathways. The application of high-throughput metabolomics offers a powerful tool to discover objective biomarkers of intake, moving the field beyond subjective reporting.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific plasma metabolites, identified through untargeted metabolomic profiling, will serve as robust objective biomarkers of habitual red meat intake, and that these metabolite patterns will be more strongly associated with the risk of developing type 2 diabetes and colorectal cancer than self-reported intake alone.
proposals.method: We will leverage stored blood samples from nested case-control studies within the NHS, NHS II, and HPFS cohorts. Using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) based untargeted metabolomics, we will profile plasma from approximately 2,000 participants (1,000 case-control pairs for diabetes and 1,000 for colorectal cancer). We will use random forest and partial least squares discriminant analysis (PLS-DA) to identify metabolites whose levels differ significantly between participants with high versus low self-reported red meat intake (validated against diet records). Significant metabolites will then be evaluated in conditional logistic regression models to test their associations with disease risk, adjusting for physical activity, BMI, and other confounders.
proposals.contribution: This work will identify a panel of objective metabolomic biomarkers for red meat consumption, improving the precision of dietary assessment in future studies. It will elucidate specific metabolic pathways (e.g., related to carnitine, choline, iron, or advanced glycation end-products) that may mediate the observed health risks. The findings will provide stronger mechanistic evidence to inform dietary guidelines regarding red meat.
proposals.novelty: While metabolomics has been applied to identify biomarkers for foods like coffee or fish, a comprehensive, large-scale discovery effort focused specifically on red meat in relation to major chronic disease endpoints is lacking. This proposal uniquely integrates objective biomarker discovery with direct testing of their predictive value for two critical health outcomes within premier prospective cohorts.

proposals.title: Agent-Based Modeling of Long-Term Population Cognitive Outcomes Under Alternative Red Meat Consumption Scenarios: A Simulation Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 evidence from prospective cohort studies, including work by Willett and others, links high red meat intake to increased risks of chronic diseases like diabetes and cardiovascular disease, which are established risk factors for cognitive decline and dementia. However, translating individual-level risk estimates into long-term population-level cognitive health outcomes remains a critical gap, limiting the ability to inform dietary guidelines and public health strategies. Agent-based modeling (ABM) offers a powerful tool to simulate these complex, dynamic population effects over time.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that simulated population-level adherence to dietary patterns with reduced red meat consumption will result in a lower cumulative incidence of cognitive impairment over a 30-year period compared to current consumption trends, mediated primarily through reductions in incident type 2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease.
proposals.method: We will develop an agent-based model using a modified ODD+D protocol, parameterized with pre-2026 data from large-scale cohorts like the Nurses' Health Study and Health Professionals Follow-up Study. Individual agents will be assigned demographic, lifestyle (including physical activity), and genetic risk profiles, with transition probabilities for developing diabetes, myocardial infarction, and cognitive decline based on published hazard ratios for red meat intake. The simulation will compare cognitive outcomes under three scenarios: 1) current consumption trends, 2) population-wide reduction to recommended levels, and 3) replacement of red meat with plant-based protein sources. The model will be implemented in NetLogo, with sensitivity analyses conducted in R.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, quantitative projection of the long-term cognitive health burden attributable to red meat consumption at the population level. It will move beyond individual risk estimates to model dynamic interactions between diet, disease incidence, and cognitive aging, offering a tool for policymakers to evaluate the potential impact of dietary interventions on future dementia rates.
proposals.novelty: While ABM has been used for infectious diseases, its application to model the long-term cognitive consequences of dietary patterns is novel. This approach uniquely integrates established dietary risk factors for multiple chronic diseases to project their compound effect on population cognitive health, a synthesis not previously attempted in nutritional epidemiology.

proposals.title: Non-linear and Interactive Effects of Planetary Health Diet Food Groups on Type 2 Diabetes Risk: An Analysis of the Nurses' Health Studies and Health Professionals Follow-up Study
proposals.motivation: Current nutritional epidemiology often models the health effects of dietary components as linear and additive. However, biological plausibility suggests that food groups within healthy dietary patterns, like the Planetary Health Diet (PHD), may interact synergistically or antagonistically. Understanding these interactions is critical for moving beyond simple dietary scores to provide more nuanced and effective dietary guidance for chronic disease prevention, particularly for type 2 diabetes, a major public health burden.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the combined intake of specific PHD food groups (e.g., whole grains and nuts, or fruits and vegetables) exhibits non-additive, synergistic associations with reduced risk of type 2 diabetes, and that these interactions are modified by levels of physical activity.
proposals.method: We will use pooled, updated data from the Nurses' Health Studies I & II and the Health Professionals Follow-up Study. Beyond standard multivariable Cox proportional hazards models, we will employ multiplicative interaction terms and likelihood ratio tests to formally test for statistical interaction. Crucially, we will implement restricted cubic splines within these models to assess non-linearity in dose-response relationships for individual and combined food intakes. Stratified analyses will be conducted by levels of physical activity to evaluate effect modification.
proposals.contribution: This work will challenge the prevailing assumption of linear additivity in dietary research. It will provide empirical evidence on whether the whole of a healthy diet is greater than the sum of its parts for diabetes prevention. The findings could refine dietary guidelines by identifying specific food combinations that maximize protective effects.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Willett and others has established the benefits of PHD components, this analysis explicitly tests for non-linear and interactive effects between them, moving beyond the standard approach of calculating a simple additive diet score. Integrating these analyses with physical activity, a key modifiable risk factor, adds a novel layer of contextual understanding.

proposals.title: Minimal Dietary Shifts for Maximum Health Benefit: An Optimization Approach to Achieve a Target Fraction of the Planetary Health Diet's Mortality Reduction in US Cohorts
proposals.motivation: The Planetary Health Diet (PHD) offers a template for sustainable, healthy eating but represents a substantial departure from current dietary patterns, creating a major barrier to adoption. Public health strategies need to identify the most efficient, least disruptive dietary changes to motivate individuals and inform policy. This problem is critical for reducing the burden of chronic diseases like type 2 diabetes and cancer, where diet is a major modifiable risk factor.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a constrained optimization framework can identify a unique set of minimal, practical dietary shifts from current intake that achieves a specified target (e.g., 50%) of the PHD's estimated mortality benefit, and that these priority shifts will differ meaningfully from a simple proportional scaling of all PHD recommendations.
proposals.method: We will use data on current dietary intake and mortality risk from large prospective cohorts (e.g., NHS, HPFS). For each participant, we will define a dietary 'disruption' metric, such as the sum of squared percentage changes in food group intakes. Using pre-2026 optimization techniques (e.g., quadratic programming with linear constraints), we will solve for the new diet that minimizes this disruption metric subject to a constraint that the predicted mortality risk reduction, based on pre-2026 disease-specific risk models, meets a target fraction of the full PHD benefit. Sensitivity analyses will vary the target fraction and disruption metric.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for prioritizing dietary recommendations based on efficiency of health gain per unit of behavioral change. It will yield a ranked set of food group adjustments for specific populations, offering actionable guidance for clinicians and public health messaging. The method bridges the gap between ideal dietary patterns and practical, personalized intervention strategies.
proposals.novelty: While previous work identifies optimal diets or adherence scores, this approach formally frames the public health challenge as a dual optimization problem—minimizing behavioral change for a fixed health gain—a perspective not previously operationalized in nutritional epidemiology. It moves beyond describing 'what' to eat to solving 'how' to get there most efficiently.

proposals.title: Effect Modification by Diabetes Status on the Association Between Alcohol Intake and Cancer Risk: A Pooled Analysis of Prospective Cohort Studies
proposals.motivation: Alcohol consumption is an established modifiable risk factor for several cancers, but its effects may be heterogeneous across populations. Pre-2026 evidence suggests diabetes is a risk factor for certain cancers and that alcohol can influence insulin sensitivity and inflammation, key pathways in carcinogenesis. Understanding whether the alcohol-cancer association differs by diabetes status is crucial for refining public health recommendations and personalized risk communication.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the positive association between alcohol intake and risk of alcohol-related cancers (e.g., breast, colorectal, liver) is stronger among individuals with prevalent or incident type 2 diabetes compared to those without diabetes, due to shared metabolic dysregulation.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pooled analysis of individual-level data from large prospective cohorts, including the Nurses' Health Studies and the Health Professionals Follow-up Study. Using Cox proportional hazards models stratified by diabetes status at baseline, we will estimate multivariable-adjusted hazard ratios for cancer per increment of alcohol intake (grams/day). We will formally test for interaction by including a product term between alcohol (continuous) and diabetes status (yes/no) and assess effect modification on both additive and multiplicative scales. Analyses will be repeated for cancer-specific endpoints and by beverage type.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive assessment of diabetes as an effect modifier of the alcohol-cancer relationship. It will clarify whether diabetic populations face a disproportionately higher cancer risk from alcohol consumption, informing more tailored dietary guidelines. The findings could reveal a synergistic biological interplay between hyperinsulinemia, chronic inflammation, and alcohol metabolism in carcinogenesis.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined alcohol and cancer or diabetes and cancer separately, the potential for diabetes to modify the alcohol-cancer risk is largely unexplored. This proposal directly tests this interaction using the detailed, repeated-measure cohort data central to Dr. Willett's research, moving beyond main effects to investigate a clinically relevant source of heterogeneity.

proposals.title: Personalized Dietary Targets for Cardiometabolic Disease Prevention: A Modeling Study Using Data from the Nurses' Health Studies and Health Professionals Follow-up Study
proposals.motivation: Current public health guidelines propose uniform dietary targets, but individuals have vastly different baseline diets and preferences, which may affect adherence and efficacy. A one-size-fits-all approach may not be optimal for risk reduction across diverse populations. This study aims to move beyond static recommendations for single foods (like SSBs or fruit) to develop a personalized framework.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that algorithmically generated, personalized dietary targets, which account for an individual's baseline diet and stated preferences, will yield a greater predicted reduction in population risk for type 2 diabetes and coronary heart disease compared to uniform targets.
proposals.method: We will use dietary, lifestyle, and health outcome data from the NHS, NHS II, and HPFS cohorts. For each participant, we will model the predicted disease risk reduction from swapping specific food items, using substitution models derived from prior cohort analyses. A constrained optimization algorithm will identify the top 2-3 personalized dietary swaps that maximize predicted risk reduction while respecting individual baseline intake and stated preference constraints (e.g., willingness to change). We will compare the aggregated predicted benefit of this personalized approach against the benefit of applying uniform population targets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept for a data-driven, personalized nutrition framework within large prospective cohorts. It will quantify the potential theoretical advantage of personalization over uniform guidelines for chronic disease prevention. The methodology could inform the design of future personalized dietary intervention trials.
proposals.novelty: While substitution models exist, applying them with optimization algorithms to generate unique, preference-aware targets for each individual in a cohort is novel. This moves beyond identifying average risk associations to operationalizing personalized dietary advice at a population scale.

proposals.title: Targeted Dietary and Physical Activity Intervention for High-Risk Adolescents: A Randomized Controlled Trial to Reduce Early Cardiometabolic Risk Markers
proposals.motivation: Adolescent obesity and poor diet quality are potent risk factors for early-onset type 2 diabetes and long-term cardiovascular disease, yet broad population-level interventions often show attenuated effects. Focusing resources on the highest-risk subgroups, where the potential for absolute risk reduction is greatest, is a critical but understudied public health strategy. This approach aligns with precision prevention principles and addresses health disparities, as these conditions disproportionately affect socioeconomically disadvantaged youth.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-component behavioral intervention targeting both diet (specifically reducing sugar-sweetened beverages and improving dietary quality) and increasing moderate-to-vigorous physical activity will yield a significantly greater reduction in insulin resistance and other cardiometabolic risk markers among high-risk adolescents (those with obesity or the poorest baseline dietary scores) compared to standard health education.
proposals.method: We propose an 18-month, school-based, cluster-randomized controlled trial. Participants will be adolescents aged 12-16 screened for obesity (BMI ≥95th percentile) or in the lowest quintile of a validated dietary quality score (e.g., AHEI-2010). The intervention arm will receive an intensive program including supervised physical activity sessions, behavioral counseling to replace sugar-sweetened beverages with water, and family-supported dietary modification. The control arm will receive standard health curriculum materials. Primary outcome will be the change in HOMA-IR. Secondary outcomes include changes in fasting glucose, lipids, blood pressure, and BMI z-score. Analysis will use an intention-to-treat approach with linear mixed models to account for clustering.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level evidence for the efficacy of a targeted, resource-efficient prevention strategy in a critically vulnerable population. It will elucidate the synergistic effects of combined dietary and physical activity modification on early pathophysiological pathways. The findings will directly inform clinical and public health guidelines for structured intervention in high-risk pediatric subgroups.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior trials that often enroll general adolescent populations, this study exclusively targets the highest-risk strata at baseline, testing a precision public health hypothesis. It integrates specific dietary targets (informed by Willett's prior work on beverages and diabetes) with structured physical activity in a unified intervention model for this subgroup.

proposals.title: Reverse Causation in the Association Between Artificially Sweetened Beverage Consumption and Type 2 Diabetes Risk: A Prospective Analysis of Health Professionals Follow-up Study Data
proposals.motivation: Observational studies consistently link artificially sweetened beverage (ASB) consumption to increased type 2 diabetes risk, but causality remains unclear. A critical, unresolved question is reverse causation: individuals at high risk for diabetes (e.g., due to weight gain or prediabetes) may switch from sugar-sweetened beverages to ASBs as a preventive measure, creating a spurious association. Clarifying this is essential for accurate dietary guidance and understanding true risk factors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of the observed positive association between ASB intake and incident type 2 diabetes in observational cohorts is attributable to reverse causation, whereby individuals with worsening cardiometabolic risk profiles increase their ASB consumption prior to diagnosis.
proposals.method: We will analyze prospective data from the Health Professionals Follow-up Study (HPFS) and the Nurses' Health Study (NHS) I & II. Using time-varying Cox proportional hazards models, we will assess the ASB-diabetes association while adjusting for detailed, repeated measures of adiposity (BMI, waist circumference), dietary patterns, and physical activity. Crucially, we will employ lagged exposure analysis (e.g., excluding the first 4 years of follow-up after ASB assessment) and conduct stratified analyses by baseline BMI and weight change trajectories to isolate the effect of reverse causation from a potential causal effect.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a rigorous, quantitative assessment of the magnitude of reverse causation bias in the ASB-diabetes literature. It will inform the interpretation of existing meta-analyses and guide the design of future studies. The findings will directly impact public health messaging by distinguishing between a harmful effect of ASBs and their use as a risk marker.
proposals.novelty: While reverse causation is often mentioned as a limitation, few studies have systematically modeled it using long-term, repeated-measures data on both ASB intake and the precise metabolic risk factors that may drive the behavioral change. This analysis directly inverts the assumed causal direction to test it empirically.

proposals.title: Frequency of Dietary Assessment and Risk of Type 2 Diabetes: Evaluating the Impact of Beverage Consumption Variability Captured by Annual vs. Quadrennial Questionnaires in the Nurses' Health Study II
proposals.motivation: Large prospective cohorts like the Nurses' Health Studies (NHS) have traditionally used quadrennial food-frequency questionnaires (FFQs) to assess long-term diet-disease associations. However, beverage consumption patterns, particularly for sugar-sweetened beverages (SSBs) and artificially sweetened beverages (ASBs), may exhibit substantial short-term variability due to marketing, policy changes, and personal weight management efforts. This variability may be misclassified by infrequent assessment, biasing risk estimates for outcomes like type 2 diabetes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that more frequent (e.g., annual) assessment of beverage intake will capture meaningful short-term variation, leading to stronger and more precise hazard ratios for the associations between SSB/ASB consumption and incident type 2 diabetes compared to estimates derived from the standard quadrennial assessment cycle.
proposals.method: We will utilize existing data from the NHS II, where participants completed FFQs every four years, but also provided annual updates on specific dietary items, including SSBs and ASBs, via supplementary questionnaires between 1991 and 2015. We will construct two primary exposure variables for cumulative average beverage intake: one using only the quadrennial FFQ data and another integrating the annual updates. Time-varying Cox proportional hazards models will be used to estimate hazard ratios for incident type 2 diabetes, adjusting for age, body mass index, physical activity, total energy intake, and other confounders. The statistical significance and magnitude of the hazard ratios from the two assessment methods will be formally compared using likelihood ratio tests.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence on the optimal frequency of dietary assessment for capturing exposure to rapidly changing food items. It will directly inform the design of future large-scale cohorts and improve the accuracy of risk estimates for beverages and diabetes, a major public health concern. The findings could validate a more efficient hybrid assessment strategy for specific dietary components.
proposals.novelty: While the limitations of FFQs are known, this analysis leverages a unique, pre-existing dataset with embedded annual updates to perform a direct, within-cohort comparison of assessment frequency, moving beyond simulation studies. It focuses specifically on a dynamic exposure (beverages) with established health risks, testing a pragmatic methodological question central to nutritional epidemiology.

proposals.title: Digital Phenotyping of Physical Activity and Beverage Consumption: A Real-Time, High-Resolution Analysis of Joint Associations with Type 2 Diabetes Risk in the Nurses' Health Study 3
proposals.motivation: Epidemiological studies of diet, physical activity, and chronic disease risk have historically relied on self-reported questionnaires, which are subject to recall bias and infrequent measurement. This limits the ability to capture complex, real-time patterns of behavior and their acute metabolic effects. Leveraging pre-2026 digital tools to obtain objective, continuous data can address these limitations and provide novel insights into behavioral risk factors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high-resolution digital phenotyping will reveal that specific temporal patterns of physical activity (e.g., postprandial movement) and beverage consumption (e.g., timing of sugar-sweetened beverage intake relative to activity) are more strongly associated with insulin resistance and incident type 2 diabetes risk than aggregate measures of total intake or activity.
proposals.method: We will recruit a sub-cohort of 5,000 participants from the ongoing Nurses' Health Study 3. For a 14-day period, participants will use a smartphone app that passively collects accelerometer data via phone sensors and prompts ecological momentary assessment (EMA) for beverage consumption. We will implement validated machine learning algorithms (e.g., Random Forest classifiers) on the accelerometer data to classify activity type and intensity. Time-series analysis will model the joint trajectories of activity and intake, which will be linked to biomarkers of glycemic control (HbA1c, fasting insulin) measured at the study's conclusion.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution, real-world evidence on how the interplay of activity and beverage consumption patterns influences diabetes risk. It will validate digital phenotyping as a scalable method for behavioral epidemiology and generate novel hypotheses about behavioral timing for disease prevention.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have used accelerometers or apps separately, this proposal integrates passive sensing with EMA in a large prospective cohort to model the dynamic, within-day interaction between two key modifiable risk factors, moving beyond static 'between-person' comparisons to 'within-person' temporal dynamics.

proposals.title: Global Burden of Type 2 Diabetes Attributable to Sugar-Sweetened Beverage Consumption: A Comparative Risk Assessment Analysis
proposals.motivation: Sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) consumption is a well-established modifiable risk factor for type 2 diabetes (T2D), with robust dose-response relationships demonstrated in large prospective cohorts. However, a comprehensive, up-to-date global assessment of the population-attributable fraction (PAF) of T2D due to SSBs is lacking. Quantifying this global burden is critical for informing international public health policy, prioritizing interventions, and tracking progress toward reducing diet-related non-communicable diseases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that SSB consumption is a major contributor to the global incidence of type 2 diabetes, with substantial variation in attributable burden across world regions, national income levels, and demographic groups.
proposals.method: We will conduct a comparative risk assessment (CRA) analysis within the Global Burden of Disease (GBD) framework. Using the dose-response risk estimates for SSBs and T2D from our recent meta-analysis of US cohorts, we will combine them with country-, age-, and sex-specific SSB consumption distributions from dietary surveys (e.g., FAO, WHO, national surveys) and the Global Dietary Database. We will apply the theoretical minimum risk exposure level (TMREL) of zero SSB intake. Attributable T2D cases and disability-adjusted life years (DALYs) will be calculated by applying the PAFs to the 2019 GBD estimates of T2D incidence and burden, using DisMod-MR 2.1 for disease modeling. Uncertainty will be propagated using Monte Carlo simulation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed, GBD-compliant global, regional, and national estimates of T2D burden attributable to SSBs. It will identify high-burden populations and quantify the potential health gains from reducing SSB intake, directly informing the World Health Organization's Global Action Plan on non-communicable diseases and national sugar-reduction policies.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have estimated national burdens, this analysis will be novel in its fully integrated use of the latest GBD 2019 T2D estimates, comprehensive global exposure data, and a consistent CRA methodology, enabling direct comparison with burdens from other risk factors and across time.

ideas.title: Culturally Adapted Dietary Transition Monitoring in Post-MI Patients Across Europe
ideas.description: This study will culturally adapt and validate the VEGANScreener for use in multiple European countries to specifically monitor dietary transitions in patients who have experienced a myocardial infarction (MI). It moves beyond simply tracking cholesterol intake to assess the holistic adoption of a plant-based dietary pattern, which is a key secondary prevention recommendation. The research will longitudinally measure how dietary patterns change after an MI event, identifying cultural, linguistic, and socioeconomic facilitators and barriers to implementing dietary advice. This provides critical, culturally-relevant data for improving post-MI nutritional counseling and support programs across Europe.

ideas.title: Machine Learning-Derived Pattern Recognition for a Whole-Food, Plant-Based Dietary Screener
ideas.description: This research applies machine learning algorithms to comprehensive dietary data from populations adhering to whole-food, plant-based (WFPB) patterns. The goal is to identify a minimal set of food groups, frequency questions, or behavioral markers that most accurately predict the overall quality and adherence to a WFPB pattern, as validated against detailed dietary records or health biomarkers. The resulting screener is not a simple nutrient checklist but a pattern-based tool derived from real-world dietary data, optimizing both predictive power and brevity. This enables efficient assessment of holistic diet quality in research and clinical settings focused on plant-based nutrition.

ideas.title: Scale to other chronic disease outcomes

ideas.title: Analyzing Post-Traumatic Behavioral Adaptation: A Signal Detection Framework for Dietary Change After MI
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from ecology and animal behavior—using signal detection theory to model behavioral change after a traumatic event—to the context of myocardial infarction (MI) survivors' dietary changes. The analogous problem of prey altering foraging behavior after a predator attack is solved by framing it as a recalibration of risk assessment, analyzing the trade-off between false alarms and missed detections. The adapted idea applies this framework to model how an MI serves as a salient signal that shifts a patient's internal threshold for consuming dietary cholesterol. By quantifying this shift and the associated trade-offs (e.g., dietary safety vs. quality of life), the study aims to predict patterns of adherence to dietary guidelines and the sustainability of behavioral change over time, moving beyond simple intake measurement to a model of perceptual and behavioral adaptation.

ideas.title: A Digital Platform for Dynamic Nutritional Phenotyping and Cholesterol Metabolism Modeling
ideas.description: This project develops a mobile/web application that integrates the dietary assessment of the VEGANScreener with a backend pharmacokinetic model of cholesterol absorption and body pool dynamics. Users input their dietary data via the screener, which then serves as the intake parameters for a personalized computational model simulating their unique cholesterol metabolism over time. This allows researchers and clinicians to move beyond static intake assessment to predicting personalized, time-dependent physiological outcomes, such as estimated plasma cholesterol changes, based on specific dietary patterns. The platform thus enables a novel form of digital nutritional phenotyping for both precision nutrition research and tailored dietary intervention planning.

ideas.title: Examine biological mediators of HPDS effect

ideas.title: Assess VEGANScreener reliability using psychometric test-retest protocols from clinical psychology
ideas.description: This idea adapts the well-established solution for assessing instrument reliability from the field of clinical psychology and psychometrics. In that field, the reliability of diagnostic or assessment questionnaires is rigorously evaluated through test-retest studies, administering the tool to the same participants at multiple time points under stable conditions and analyzing agreement with statistics like Intraclass Correlation Coefficients and Cohen's Kappa. Adapted to this context, the research would involve recruiting a cohort of individuals with stable vegan dietary patterns, administering the VEGANScreener at two time points (e.g., 2-4 weeks apart), and using these same statistical protocols to quantify the tool's temporal stability, reproducibility, and reliability for both total scores and individual items.

ideas.title: A Dual-Classifier Screener: Integrating Food Processing Level with Nutrient Profile for Targeted Dietary Guidance
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a dietary assessment screener that combines two classification axes to identify the highest-priority foods for reduction. The first axis categorizes foods by their level of industrial processing, while the second scores them based on key nutrient limits aligned with public health guidelines. The screener's algorithm generates a priority score, focusing intervention messages on foods that are both ultra-processed and nutritionally poor. This integrated approach overcomes the limitation of using either metric alone, ensuring the tool targets dietary changes with the strongest expected benefit for health outcomes.

ideas.title: Scale up to global burden of disease analysis
ideas.description: Estimate worldwide diabetes cases attributable to SSB consumption using this study's risk estimates.
ideas.sourceTitle: Sugar-sweetened or artificially sweetened beverage consumption, physical activity and risk of type 2 diabetes in US adults

ideas.title: IRT-Optimized Dynamic Nutritional Phenotyping for Personalized Diet Response
ideas.description: This research fuses psychometric and pharmacokinetic modeling to develop a new class of adaptive nutritional assessment tools. It applies Item Response Theory (IRT) to dynamically refine and score short dietary screeners, but uses a person's longitudinal physiological data—modeled via pharmacokinetic principles to track nutrient absorption and body pool dynamics (e.g., cholesterol)—as the 'gold standard' criterion for IRT calibration. This creates a closed-loop system where the screener items and their scoring weights are statistically optimized not against static population intake data, but against each individual's unique, model-inferred physiological response to diet. The result is a personalized phenotyping tool that becomes increasingly precise at predicting an individual's disease-risk-related metabolic shifts from simple dietary queries.

ideas.title: Context-Embedded Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic-Nutritional Modeling for Personalized Cardiovascular Risk Stratification
ideas.description: This research develops a modular, multi-scale simulation platform that integrates a core pharmacokinetic model of dietary cholesterol absorption and tissue distribution with critical physiological context modules. These modules account for genetic predispositions, gut microbiome metabolic activity, hepatic feedback regulation, and systemic inflammatory states to dynamically personalize the model. The system is calibrated and validated using existing longitudinal cohort data linking detailed dietary records, biomarker panels, and cardiovascular outcomes. The final output is a validated simulation tool that moves beyond simple intake-to-biomarker links to predict personalized, dynamic disease risk trajectories based on an individual's unique physiological context and dietary patterns.

ideas.title: Goal-Aligned Dietary & Supplemental Optimization (GADSO) Screener
ideas.description: This research develops a personalized dietary assessment tool where 'quality' is defined by the degree to which an individual's total nutrient intake—from both whole foods and intentional supplements—achieves biomarker or clinical targets associated with their personal health objectives (e.g., managing blood pressure, improving athletic recovery, supporting pregnancy). It moves beyond treating supplementation as a generic positive indicator, instead evaluating specific supplement use for its efficacy in closing identified nutrient gaps relative to the user's goal physiology. This creates a novel, goal-oriented nutritional sufficiency metric that validates or challenges supplement regimens based on personalized need rather than general population guidelines.

ideas.title: Isotope-Grounded Nutritional Metabolomics for Disentangling Dietary Cholesterol Biomarkers
ideas.description: This redesigned system integrates a controlled feeding study with dual-isotope tracers to provide an objective ground truth for dietary cholesterol intake, overcoming the fundamental confounding of endogenous synthesis. High-throughput metabolomic profiling is performed on samples from participants consuming precisely measured, isotopically labeled cholesterol, enabling models to identify metabolite patterns directly linked to the dietary tracer. The concurrent measurement of endogenous synthesis rates via a second tracer allows the model to account for and correct inter-individual metabolic variation. The result is a robust, validated panel of biomarkers that specifically reflect dietary cholesterol intake, independent of internal production.

ideas.title: Personalized Dietary Pharmacokinetics via Expert-Preference-Constrained Modeling
ideas.description: This research fuses conjoint analysis and pharmacokinetic modeling to develop a novel framework for personalized nutrition. Expert-derived preference weights for dietary components (e.g., saturated fat, fiber type) are used to constrain and inform the parameter estimation of dynamic models of nutrient absorption and body pool kinetics (e.g., for cholesterol). The synergy produces 'behaviorally-validated' physiological models that predict individual health outcomes based on intake, where the model structure inherently reflects expert valuations of which dietary factors matter most and how they interact dynamically, bridging the gap between qualitative clinical wisdom and quantitative systems biology.

ideas.title: Apply Instrumental Variable Methods from Econometrics to Causal Inference in Nutritional Epidemiology
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from econometrics, where instrumental variable (IV) methods are a cornerstone for causal inference in non-experimental settings. The analogous solution involves using carefully chosen instruments (like policy shocks) that are exogenous and affect an outcome only through a specific exposure, allowing the estimation of causal effects. Adapting this to nutritional epidemiology, we propose to formally apply the rigorous IV validation framework from econometrics to Mendelian randomization studies. Specifically, for using genetic variants as instruments to infer causality for dietary cholesterol, we would adapt econometric tests for instrument strength, exogeneity, and the exclusion restriction to better assess and mitigate biases like pleiotropy, thereby providing more robust and defensible causal estimates of dietary cholesterol's impact on cardiovascular disease and other health outcomes.

ideas.title: A 'Whole-Food Vegan Pattern' Screener Using Ecological Community Analysis
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that nutritional adequacy is best assessed by tracking isolated nutrients. By drawing an analogy from ecology, where the health of an ecosystem is evaluated by the structure and function of its species community rather than individual species counts, we adapt this solution to nutrition. The research will develop a dietary screener that evaluates a whole-food vegan pattern based on the diversity, evenness, and compositional balance of core plant-food groups (e.g., legumes, whole grains, fruits, vegetables, nuts/seeds). This approach shifts the focus from nutrient-level data to a holistic, pattern-based assessment, analogous to how an ecologist characterizes a robust biological community.

ideas.title: A Food Processing 'Carcinogen Classification' System for Dietary Screening
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from toxicology—the IARC carcinogen classification system—to challenge the assumption that dietary screening must focus on specific nutrient limits. In toxicology, agents are grouped by the strength of evidence for harm (carcinogenicity) rather than by tracking exposure to every constituent chemical. Analogously, we propose creating a dietary screener that classifies foods into a tiered system based on their level and purpose of industrial processing (e.g., NOVA groups). The screener would then emphasize reducing cumulative consumption from the highest processing tiers (particularly ultra-processed foods), providing a simpler, more holistic metric of diet quality that inherently captures clusters of undesirable nutrients and food properties.

ideas.title: Adapt Discrete Choice Experiments from Environmental Economics to Weight Dietary Preferences
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Environmental Economics, where Discrete Choice Experiments (DCEs) are used to quantify public preferences for non-market environmental goods by analyzing trade-offs in hypothetical choice sets. The analogous solution is applied to the research context of dietary preference weighting by designing DCEs where nutrition and public health experts are presented with repeated choices between hypothetical diets or food policies. Each option is defined by key attributes (e.g., grams of protein, carbon footprint, cost per serving, processing level). By forcing experts to make trade-offs, their choices statistically reveal the latent utility and relative importance they assign to each dietary component, creating a quantitatively rigorous preference-weighting system derived from expert judgment rather than market data.

ideas.title: Fairness-Constrained Multi-Cohort ML for Generalizable Predictive Diet Screening
ideas.description: This research develops a machine learning system to identify short dietary screener items that are predictive, generalizable, and fair. It trains models simultaneously on multiple diverse population cohorts, applying adversarial debiasing and fairness constraints during the feature selection process to minimize demographic bias. The final output is a validated, short-form screener whose predictive performance for key dietary patterns is robust and equitable across different subpopulations, mitigating the risks of algorithmic bias inherent in single-dataset approaches.

ideas.title: Applying Hormetic Dose-Response Models to Cholesterol and Myocardial Infarction
ideas.description: This idea challenges the linearity assumption in the cholesterol-MI relationship by drawing an analogy from toxicology, where the linear no-threshold model for toxins was successfully challenged by evidence of hormetic (J-shaped) dose-responses. The analogous solution involved experimental designs with broad dose ranges and non-linear statistical modeling to detect thresholds and adaptive benefits. Adapted to this context, the research would involve a cohort study with granular cholesterol data across an extreme range, employing flexible statistical techniques like segmented regression to test for and characterize a potential threshold or J-shaped curve, thereby questioning a foundational assumption in cardiovascular risk prediction.

ideas.title: Contextual Rule-Elicitation Hybrid for Uncovering Dietary Decision Heuristics
ideas.description: This method moves beyond standard conjoint analysis by explicitly modeling the non-compensatory rules and contextual factors that govern real dietary choices. It combines an adaptive discrete choice experiment, which respects participant-stated absolute constraints (like allergies or ethical exclusions), with in-depth retrospective interviews to uncover the heuristics and situational drivers behind each choice. The resulting output is a set of contextually qualified preference weights, paired with a decision-rule map that shows when and why different dietary components become salient or non-negotiable for experts.

ideas.title: Apply Feature Selection from Seismology for Predictive Diet Screener Development
ideas.description: This idea adapts a machine learning solution from geophysics to the problem of creating short dietary screeners. In seismology, ML is used to analyze complex waveform data to identify the minimal, most predictive features for earthquake early warning. Similarly, this research will apply ML algorithms (e.g., LASSO, Random Forest) to comprehensive dietary intake data to model a target outcome, such as a validated diet quality score or health risk. By analyzing the feature importance or coefficients from the trained model, the research will objectively identify which specific food items or short questions carry the most predictive power. This data-driven method adapts the seismological feature selection paradigm to derive an optimized, brief dietary assessment tool from a larger, more complex instrument.

ideas.title: Use a machine learning classifier trained on metabolic fingerprints to quantify cholesterol intake
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from microbial source tracking in environmental science to the problem of objectively measuring dietary cholesterol intake. In microbial ecology, classifiers are trained on libraries of known source profiles (e.g., human vs. animal feces) to quantify contamination sources in complex water samples. Adapted to nutritional metabolomics, the research would involve creating a controlled feeding study to generate a high-throughput metabolomics reference library correlating specific plasma or urine metabolic fingerprints with precisely known levels of cholesterol intake. A machine learning model would be trained on this library to learn the multi-metabolite signature of cholesterol consumption. This model could then be deployed to analyze metabolomic profiles from cohort studies, providing a powerful, objective, and quantitative biomarker of individual cholesterol intake that captures the complexity of metabolic response better than a single compound.

ideas.title: Scale down to precise biological mechanisms using a tracer isotope approach
ideas.description: This idea adapts the stable isotope tracer methodology from soil and plant nutrient cycling ecology to solve the problem of scaling down from population-level lipoprotein measurements to precise biological mechanisms. In ecology, applying 15N-labeled fertilizer allows researchers to trace the exact flow and transformation of nitrogen through complex biotic and abiotic pools. Adapted to the original RCT context, this involves conducting controlled cholesterol feeding trials using deuterium- or 13C-labeled cholesterol and specific fatty acids. By sequentially measuring the isotopic enrichment in plasma lipoprotein subfractions (VLDL, LDL, HDL), bile acids, and other compartments, researchers can directly map the kinetic pathways—such as intestinal absorption, hepatic secretion, lipoprotein remodeling, and clearance rates—that underlie the net changes in lipoprotein concentrations, moving beyond correlation to definitive mechanistic insight.

ideas.title: Adapting Hydrological Catchment Modeling to Map Dietary Cholesterol Flux
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from hydrology, where dynamic catchment models simulate water flow through landscape pools, to the problem of modeling cholesterol absorption and body pools. In the analogous field, inputs (rainfall) are tracked through interconnected compartments (snow, soil, groundwater) using transfer functions to predict system outputs (streamflow). The adapted research idea treats the human body as a 'nutritional catchment,' where dietary cholesterol intake is the input. Compartmental pools (e.g., gut lumen, enterocytes, lymph, plasma lipoproteins, liver, tissues) are defined, and mass-balance equations with biologically informed transfer rates (e.g., mediated by NPC1L1, ACAT2, CETP) are used to dynamically simulate cholesterol flux. This model would link variable dietary intake to predicted plasma cholesterol concentrations and tissue deposition over time, incorporating individual-specific 'watershed characteristics' like genetic polymorphisms and gut microbiome composition to personalize disease risk assessment.

ideas.title: Applying Survival Analysis and Landmarking from Oncology to Model Long-Term Post-MI Risk
ideas.description: This idea proposes extending MI risk analysis beyond a mean 3.5-year follow-up by drawing an analogy to long-term survivorship research in clinical oncology. In that field, solutions like landmark analysis and complex time-varying covariate models are used to dissect distinct risk phases and evolving patient profiles over decades. Adapted to cardiology, the research would implement a landmark analysis at the original study's median follow-up to specifically model the long-term risk trajectory for patients who survive the initial high-risk period. Furthermore, by employing extended survival models that incorporate time-dependent variables—such as changes in medication, occurrence of other health events, and aging—the study can provide a dynamic and accurate assessment of the cumulative effects and evolving risk factors for myocardial infarction over 10 or more years, moving beyond a simple extension of observation time.

ideas.title: Testing Ecological Validity: Adapting Model Organism Translation Methods to Human Population Generalizability
ideas.description: This idea uses an analogy from preclinical biomedical research, where a fundamental problem is the limited generalizability of findings from standard animal models (like young male mice) to other populations (like females or genetically diverse strains). The analogous solution involves prospective, systematic testing across deliberately diverse models. Adapted to the original research seed, this means moving beyond simply suggesting the association be tested in other groups. Instead, the research should be explicitly designed from the outset as a multi-cohort validation study. The veteran population is treated as the initial 'discovery cohort' or 'standard model.' Parallel, methodologically matched cohorts of non-veterans, younger individuals, and females are then prospectively recruited as distinct 'validation models.' Analytical techniques inspired by comparative studies across diverse animal strains (e.g., testing for interaction effects by cohort) are used to rigorously assess whether the observed association holds uniformly or demonstrates significant heterogeneity across these human demographic and experiential strata, thereby directly challenging the assumption of generalizability from the original sample.

ideas.title: Apply Topological Data Analysis to Discover Dietary Patterns Linked to Myocardial Infarction
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from astrophysics, where Topological Data Analysis (TDA) is used to uncover the complex filamentary structure of the cosmic web from noisy galaxy survey data. The analogous problem in nutritional epidemiology is discovering meaningful, non-linear dietary patterns involving cholesterol and related factors within high-dimensional, noisy consumption data. The adapted research idea applies TDA, specifically persistent homology, to cohort dietary data to identify robust topological features (like clusters or voids in dietary space) that represent distinct dietary regimes. The persistence and characteristics of these topologically-defined patterns will then be tested as predictors for incident myocardial infarction, offering a novel, geometry-driven method for pattern discovery beyond traditional statistical or machine learning clustering.

ideas.title: Causal Transportability and Measurement Invariance Framework for Cross-Population Generalizability
ideas.description: This study moves beyond simple association testing to formally evaluate whether the causal structure and effect sizes of the original finding are transportable to non-veteran, younger, and female populations. It first establishes measurement invariance to ensure latent constructs are comparable across groups, then employs causal diagrams to identify which causal pathways are transportable. The analysis tests for effect heterogeneity and uses transportability formulas to adjust for population-specific confounders, providing a robust assessment of whether the underlying mechanism—not just the statistical association—generalizes beyond the original veteran cohort.

ideas.title: Multi-state modeling of post-MI event pathways with competing risk adjustment in extended follow-up
ideas.description: This study implements a prospective, protocol-driven long-term follow-up using a multi-state modeling framework to overcome biases inherent in simply extending observation time. The analytical plan pre-specifies states (e.g., alive and event-free, recurrent MI, cardiovascular death, non-cardiovascular death) and permissible transitions, with data collection intervals defined to minimize immortal time bias. The primary aim is to estimate state occupancy probabilities and cumulative incidence functions for key clinical events, properly accounting for competing risks. This provides a robust, interpretable quantification of long-term patient trajectories beyond mean follow-up times.

ideas.description: This approach integrates structural validation directly into the IRT optimization pipeline. Before refining items with IRT, the system rigorously tests the dimensionality of the screener's target construct using factor analysis. Based on the results, it then applies an appropriate psychometric model—either a multidimensional IRT framework for complex constructs or a unidimensional IRT model for a validated single trait. This ensures that the statistical optimization of items and scoring is built on a valid representation of the underlying construct, dramatically improving measurement accuracy and diagnostic utility.

ideas.title: Adapting Signal Detection Theory from Sensory Neuroscience for Screener Refinement
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core methodological framework from sensory neuroscience—Signal Detection Theory (SDT)—to the problem of screener refinement. In its original field, SDT quantifies a system's ability to distinguish a target signal from noise, separating the inherent sensitivity (d') from the observer's decision criterion. By applying this analogy, we can model a psychological or medical screener's performance by statistically separating its discriminative power (its ability to correctly identify true cases) from the applied scoring cutoff. This adaptation provides a robust method to optimize individual screener items and overall scoring, allowing researchers to explicitly tune the instrument for maximum sensitivity, specificity, or a balanced trade-off depending on the clinical or research context, much like adjusting a detection threshold in a psychophysical experiment.
